description: Functions of the form f(x) = mx + b where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept, producing straight-line graphs.
hint: The coefficient of x tells you how steep the line is, and the constant tells you where it crosses the y-axis.

description: Functions formed by sums of power functions with non-negative integer exponents, written as f(x) = aₙxⁿ + aₙ₋₁xⁿ⁻¹ + ... + a₁x + a₀.
hint: The highest power (degree) determines the end behavior and maximum number of turning points.

description: Functions expressed as the ratio of two polynomials, f(x) = p(x)/q(x), where q(x) ≠ 0.
hint: Look for values where the denominator equals zero — these create vertical asymptotes or holes.

description: Functions constructed using algebraic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, roots) on polynomials.
hint: If you see a root symbol or fractional exponent combined with polynomials, you likely have an algebraic function.

description: Functions of the form f(x) = bˣ where b > 0, b ≠ 1, with the variable in the exponent.
hint: The base stays constant while the exponent varies — this creates rapid growth (b > 1) or decay (0 < b < 1).

description: Functions of the form f(x) = logᵦ(x), the inverse of exponential functions, answering 'what power gives x?'
hint: Remember logᵦ(x) asks 'b to what power equals x?' and is only defined for positive x.

description: Functions including sine, cosine, tangent and their reciprocals, originally defined from right triangle ratios and extended to all real numbers.
hint: These functions repeat their values in regular intervals (periods) — use the unit circle for angles beyond 0 to 90 degrees.

description: Functions of the form f(x) = xⁿ where n is any real number, with the variable as the base.
hint: Unlike exponentials, here the base changes while the exponent stays fixed — even powers create parabola-like shapes.

description: Functions defined by different formulas on different intervals of the domain, often written with cases.
hint: Evaluate by first determining which interval your x-value falls in, then use only that formula.

description: The function f(x) = |x| that returns the distance from zero, creating a V-shaped graph.
hint: Split into cases: |x| = x when x ≥ 0, and |x| = -x when x < 0.

description: The value that f(x) approaches as x approaches a specific value, denoted lim(x→a) f(x) = L.
hint: Focus on where f(x) is heading as x gets close to a, not necessarily what f(a) equals.

description: Limits approached from only the left (x→a⁻) or only the right (x→a⁺) of a point.
hint: Check behavior separately from each direction; left-hand and right-hand limits may differ at discontinuities.

description: A limit exists at x = a if and only if both one-sided limits exist and are equal.
hint: If lim(x→a⁻) f(x) ≠ lim(x→a⁺) f(x), the two-sided limit does not exist.

description: Approximating limits by evaluating f(x) at values progressively closer to the target point.
hint: Create a table with x-values approaching from both sides and observe the pattern in f(x) values.

description: Determining limits by examining the height a graph approaches as x nears a point.
hint: Trace along the curve toward the point; look at the y-coordinate the graph is heading toward, ignoring holes or jumps.

description: Behavior of limits at points where the function has holes, jumps, or vertical asymptotes.
hint: The limit can exist even when f(a) is undefined; check if both sides approach the same finite value.

description: When f(x) grows without bound (approaches ±∞) as x approaches a finite value.
hint: Look for vertical asymptotes; if f(x) increases or decreases without bound, write lim = ∞ or -∞.

description: The symbolic representation lim(x→a) f(x) = L, including variants for one-sided and infinite limits.
hint: Read 'lim(x→a) f(x) = L' as 'f(x) approaches L as x approaches a'; the arrow shows direction.

description: Evaluating limits at boundary points where the function formula changes.
hint: Apply the appropriate piece formula for each one-sided limit, then check if they match.

description: A function is a rule that assigns each element in the domain exactly one element in the range, written as f(x) or y = f(x).
hint: Check if each input value produces exactly one output value; vertical line test works on graphs.

description: The domain is the set of all possible input values for a function, while the range is the set of all possible output values.
hint: For domain, find x-values that don't cause division by zero or negatives under even roots; range follows from domain.

description: Function composition (f ∘ g)(x) = f(g(x)) means applying one function to the result of another function.
hint: Work from inside out: first evaluate the inner function g(x), then use that result as input for f.

description: A function is even if f(-x) = f(x) for all x (symmetric about y-axis) and odd if f(-x) = -f(x) (symmetric about origin).
hint: Substitute -x into the function and simplify; compare the result to the original function or its negative.

description: A graph represents a function if and only if no vertical line intersects the graph more than once.
hint: Imagine or draw vertical lines through the graph; if any line hits two or more points, it's not a function.

description: Transformations including vertical/horizontal shifts, reflections, and stretching/compression modify the graph of a parent function.
hint: Changes inside f(x) affect horizontal direction (opposite of what you expect); changes outside affect vertical direction directly.

description: A function f has an inverse f⁻¹ if f⁻¹(f(x)) = x and f(f⁻¹(x)) = x; graphically, inverse functions reflect over the line y = x.
hint: To find an inverse algebraically, swap x and y then solve for y; only one-to-one functions have inverses.

description: A function is one-to-one if each output value corresponds to exactly one input value, verifiable by the horizontal line test.
hint: Use the horizontal line test: if any horizontal line intersects the graph more than once, the function is not one-to-one.

description: An angle measurement where one radian is the angle subtended by an arc length equal to the radius of a circle.
hint: Remember that π radians equals 180 degrees, so multiply degrees by π/180 to convert to radians.

description: The definition of trigonometric functions using coordinates of points on a circle of radius 1 centered at the origin.
hint: For angle θ, the point (cos θ, sin θ) lies on the unit circle, making the x-coordinate cosine and y-coordinate sine.

description: The fundamental trigonometric functions where sin θ gives the y-coordinate and cos θ gives the x-coordinate on the unit circle.
hint: Sketch the unit circle and mark the angle from the positive x-axis to find the coordinates directly.

description: Trigonometric functions defined as tan θ = sin θ/cos θ and cot θ = cos θ/sin θ.
hint: Tangent is undefined when cosine equals zero (at odd multiples of π/2), so check the denominator first.

description: Reciprocal trigonometric functions defined as sec θ = 1/cos θ and csc θ = 1/sin θ.
hint: These functions are undefined wherever their reciprocals equal zero, creating vertical asymptotes.

description: The property that trigonometric functions repeat their values at regular intervals, with sine and cosine having period 2π.
hint: Use the fact that sin(θ + 2π) = sin θ to simplify expressions by subtracting multiples of 2π from large angles.

description: Fundamental trigonometric identities including sin²θ + cos²θ = 1, derived from the unit circle.
hint: When you see sin²θ or cos²θ, try substituting using sin²θ = 1 - cos²θ to simplify expressions.

description: The visual representations of sine, cosine, and tangent functions showing their periodic behavior and key features like amplitude and period.
hint: Start by plotting key points at 0, π/2, π, 3π/2, and 2π, then connect them with the appropriate wave shape.

id: Domain and Range of Trigonometric Functions
description: The sets of allowable inputs and corresponding outputs for each trigonometric function, with sine and cosine having range [-1, 1].
hint: Check where denominators become zero to find excluded domain values for tangent, cotangent, secant, and cosecant.

description: A function is one-to-one if every horizontal line intersects its graph at most once.
hint: Draw horizontal lines across the graph; if any line hits the curve twice, the function is not one-to-one.

description: A function f⁻¹ that reverses the mapping of f, where f⁻¹(f(x)) = x and f(f⁻¹(x)) = x.
hint: To find an inverse, swap x and y in the equation, then solve for y.

description: The domain of f⁻¹ equals the range of f, and the range of f⁻¹ equals the domain of f.
hint: If (a,b) is on the graph of f, then (b,a) is on the graph of f⁻¹.

description: The graph of f⁻¹ is the reflection of the graph of f across the line y = x.
hint: Plot several points on f, then swap coordinates to get points on f⁻¹.

description: A non-one-to-one function can be made invertible by restricting its domain to a one-to-one portion.
hint: For functions like x², restrict to x ≥ 0 to make it one-to-one.

description: Verify two functions are inverses by showing f(g(x)) = x and g(f(x)) = x for all appropriate x.
hint: Compose both ways and simplify completely; both compositions must equal x.

description: To find f⁻¹, replace f(x) with y, swap x and y, then solve for y in terms of x.
hint: Start by writing y = f(x), exchange x and y, then isolate the new y.

description: Functions like arcsin, arccos, and arctan that reverse trigonometric functions on restricted domains.
hint: Remember arcsin has range [-π/2, π/2] and arccos has range [0, π].

description: Exponential functions have form f(x) = b^x where b > 0, b ≠ 1, with domain all reals and range (0,∞).
hint: Remember the base must be positive and not equal to 1; sketch y = 2^x to see why range is always positive.

description: The function f(x) = e^x uses Euler's number e ≈ 2.718 as the base, appearing naturally in growth and decay.
hint: Think of e^x as the 'natural' choice for continuous growth; its derivative equals itself.

description: Models of form f(t) = A·b^t describe growth (b > 1) or decay (0 < b < 1) over time.
hint: Check whether your base is greater than or less than 1 to determine if quantity increases or decreases.

description: The logarithm log_b(x) is the inverse function of b^x, answering 'what power gives x?'
hint: If you're stuck on log_2(8), ask yourself '2 to what power equals 8?'

description: The natural logarithm ln(x) = log_e(x) is the inverse of e^x with domain (0,∞) and range all reals.
hint: Remember ln and e^x undo each other: ln(e^x) = x and e^(ln x) = x for valid inputs.

description: Key properties include log_b(xy) = log_b(x) + log_b(y), log_b(x/y) = log_b(x) - log_b(y), and log_b(x^r) = r·log_b(x).
hint: Use these properties to break complex logarithms into simpler pieces; multiplication inside becomes addition outside.

description: The formula log_b(x) = ln(x)/ln(b) converts any logarithm to natural logarithms for calculation.
hint: When your calculator only has ln, use this formula to compute log_b(x) by dividing two natural logs.

description: Exponential graphs pass through (0,1), are always positive, increase for b > 1, decrease for 0 < b < 1, with horizontal asymptote y = 0.
hint: Plot the key point (0,1) first, then check if your base makes the function rise or fall as x increases.

description: Logarithmic graphs pass through (1,0), have domain (0,∞), vertical asymptote x = 0, and are reflections of exponential graphs over y = x.
hint: Start by plotting (1,0) and drawing the vertical asymptote at x = 0; remember the graph only exists for positive x.

description: Finding the slope of a line that touches a curve at exactly one point without crossing it nearby.
hint: Start by calculating slopes of secant lines between the point of tangency and nearby points, then observe what happens as the nearby point gets closer.

description: A line through two points on a curve that approximates the tangent line as the points move closer together.
hint: Calculate the slope using the difference quotient (f(x+h)-f(x))/h where h is the horizontal distance between points.

description: The rate at which a function is changing at a specific instant, found by taking the limit of average rates of change.
hint: Think of this as your speedometer reading at one moment, not average speed over a trip—use limits to find it from average rates.

description: The region between a function and the x-axis over an interval, calculated by summing infinitely many infinitesimally thin rectangles.
hint: Start by approximating with a finite number of rectangles, then increase the number of rectangles and observe the pattern as their width approaches zero.

description: A method of approximating area under a curve by summing the areas of rectangles with heights determined by function values.
hint: Divide your interval into n equal parts, calculate rectangle areas using function values at chosen points, then sum them up.

description: The branch of calculus concerned with rates of change, slopes of curves, and derivatives.
hint: Focus on how limits transform the tangent line problem into a systematic method for finding instantaneous rates.

description: The branch of calculus concerned with accumulation, areas, and antiderivatives.
hint: Recognize that finding area under curves requires summing infinitely many pieces, which is accomplished through limits.

description: The limit of a sum equals the sum of the limits: lim[f(x) + g(x)] = lim f(x) + lim g(x).
hint: Find the limit of each function separately, then add the results together.

description: The limit of a difference equals the difference of the limits: lim[f(x) - g(x)] = lim f(x) - lim g(x).
hint: Evaluate each limit individually, then subtract the second from the first.

description: The limit of a constant times a function equals the constant times the limit: lim[c·f(x)] = c·lim f(x).
hint: Factor out the constant, find the limit of the remaining function, then multiply by the constant.

description: The limit of a product equals the product of the limits: lim[f(x)·g(x)] = lim f(x)·lim g(x).
hint: Calculate each limit separately, then multiply the results together.

description: The limit of a quotient equals the quotient of the limits when the denominator limit is nonzero: lim[f(x)/g(x)] = lim f(x)/lim g(x), provided lim g(x) ≠ 0.
hint: Check that the denominator's limit is nonzero before dividing the limits separately.

description: The limit of a power equals the power of the limit: lim[f(x)]^n = [lim f(x)]^n for any positive integer n.
hint: Find the limit of the base function first, then raise that result to the given power.

description: The limit of a root equals the root of the limit: lim[f(x)]^(1/n) = [lim f(x)]^(1/n) when the root is defined.
hint: Evaluate the limit inside the radical first, then take the nth root if it exists.

description: For polynomial functions, the limit as x approaches a equals direct substitution: lim(x→a) p(x) = p(a).
hint: For polynomials, simply substitute the value of a into the function to find the limit.

description: For rational functions p(x)/q(x), if q(a) ≠ 0, then lim(x→a) p(x)/q(x) = p(a)/q(a) by direct substitution.
hint: Substitute the value directly, but first verify the denominator does not equal zero at that point.

description: Complex limit problems require sequential application of multiple limit laws in the correct order.
hint: Break down complex expressions into simpler parts, apply the appropriate law to each part, then combine results.

description: A function f is continuous at point a if lim(x→a) f(x) = f(a), requiring the limit exists, f(a) is defined, and they are equal.
hint: Check three things: does f(a) exist, does the limit exist, and are they the same value?

description: Discontinuities are classified as removable (hole), jump (different one-sided limits), or infinite (vertical asymptote).
hint: Draw the graph and look for holes (removable), jumps (different left/right limits), or vertical asymptotes (infinite).

description: A function is continuous from the right at a if lim(x→a+) f(x) = f(a), and from the left if lim(x→a-) f(x) = f(a).
hint: For endpoints of intervals, check only the appropriate one-sided limit equals the function value.

description: A function is continuous on an interval if it is continuous at every point in that interval, using appropriate one-sided limits at endpoints.
hint: Check continuity at each interior point and use one-sided continuity at any endpoints.

id: Continuity of Polynomial and Rational Functions
description: Polynomial functions are continuous everywhere; rational functions are continuous on their domains (wherever the denominator is nonzero).
hint: Polynomials never have discontinuities; for rational functions, just avoid division by zero.

description: If g is continuous at a and f is continuous at g(a), then the composition f∘g is continuous at a.
hint: Check the inner function's continuity first, then check the outer function at the inner function's output value.

description: If f is continuous on [a,b] and N is between f(a) and f(b), then there exists c in (a,b) where f(c) = N.
hint: To show an equation has a solution, find two points where the function is above and below your target value.

description: A removable discontinuity at a can be eliminated by redefining f(a) to equal lim(x→a) f(x).
hint: Calculate the limit at the discontinuity; if it exists, redefine the function value to match that limit.

description: A limit L exists at c if for every ε > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that |f(x) - L| < ε whenever 0 < |x - c| < δ.
hint: Draw vertical bands at L ± ε and find a horizontal band around c that keeps outputs within those bounds.
section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

description: Epsilon (ε) represents how close we want f(x) to be to the limit value L.
hint: Think of epsilon as your acceptable error: smaller epsilon means you demand the function gets closer to L.
section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

description: Delta (δ) represents how close x must be to c to guarantee f(x) is within ε of L.
hint: After choosing epsilon, solve the inequality |f(x) - L| < ε for |x - c| to find a suitable delta.
section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

description: Formally proving a limit involves showing that for any given ε, you can find a corresponding δ that satisfies the definition.
hint: Start by assuming ε is given, manipulate |f(x) - L| < ε algebraically to isolate |x - c|, then choose δ based on your work.
section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

description: One-sided limits use the epsilon-delta definition with the restriction that x approaches c from only one direction.
hint: For right-hand limits use 0 < x - c < δ, and for left-hand limits use 0 < c - x < δ in your proof.
section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

description: The epsilon-delta definition creates a rectangular region where if x is within δ of c, then f(x) stays within ε of L.
hint: Sketch horizontal lines at y = L ± ε and find vertical lines at x = c ± δ that keep the graph between the horizontal lines.
section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

description: The value of δ typically depends on the chosen ε, often requiring δ to be smaller as ε decreases.
hint: When proving limits, express delta as a function of epsilon, like δ = ε/2 or δ = min(1, ε/3).
section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

description: The limit of f(x) as x approaches c is infinity if for every M > 0, there exists δ > 0 such that f(x) > M whenever 0 < |x - c| < δ.
hint: Replace the ε condition with requiring f(x) to exceed any large value M when x is close to c.
section: 2.5 The Precise Definition of a Limit

description: The ratio of change in function values to change in input values over an interval, computed as [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h.
hint: Draw a secant line connecting two points on the curve and find its slope using rise over run.

description: The expression [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h that measures average rate of change and forms the basis of the derivative definition.
hint: Substitute x+h into the function, subtract f(x), then divide everything by h before taking the limit.

description: The limit of the difference quotient as h approaches 0, representing the instantaneous rate of change at a specific x-value.
hint: Simplify the difference quotient algebraically to cancel the h in the denominator before applying the limit.

description: Symbols f'(x), dy/dx, df/dx, or d/dx[f(x)] all representing the derivative as a function of x.
hint: The prime notation f'(x) and Leibniz notation dy/dx are interchangeable ways to denote the same derivative.

description: A function is differentiable at x=a if the limit defining f'(a) exists and is finite.
hint: Check that both left-hand and right-hand limits of the difference quotient exist and are equal.

description: If a function is differentiable at a point, it must also be continuous there, but continuity does not guarantee differentiability.
hint: Remember that sharp corners and vertical tangents break differentiability even when the function is continuous.

description: The derivative f'(a) gives the slope of the tangent line to the curve at x=a, used in point-slope form.
hint: Use the point-slope formula y - f(a) = f'(a)(x - a) with the derivative as the slope.

description: When position is s(t), the derivative s'(t) represents instantaneous velocity at time t.
hint: Position's rate of change is velocity; differentiate the position function to find how fast location changes.

description: The derivative f'(x) is defined as the limit of the difference quotient as h approaches zero for all x where the limit exists.
hint: Start with the difference quotient [f(x+h)-f(x)]/h and take the limit as h→0, treating x as a variable.

description: A function is differentiable on an interval if its derivative exists at every point in that interval.
hint: Check whether the derivative limit exists at each point; use one-sided derivatives at endpoints.

description: Points where a function has corners, cusps, vertical tangents, or discontinuities are non-differentiable.
hint: Look for sharp turns, breaks, or vertical slopes where the left and right derivative limits don't match.

description: The second derivative f''(x) is the derivative of f'(x), and higher derivatives follow by repeated differentiation.
hint: Apply the derivative operation multiple times: differentiate the first derivative to get the second.

description: The notations f'(x), dy/dx, df/dx, and Dx[f] all represent the same derivative operation.
hint: Prime notation f'(x) is convenient for functions; Leibniz notation dy/dx emphasizes the rate of change interpretation.

description: The graph of f'(x) shows the slope of f(x) at each point, revealing where f is increasing, decreasing, or has horizontal tangents.
hint: Where f has a horizontal tangent, f' crosses zero; steep sections of f correspond to large absolute values of f'.

description: For f(x) = x^n, the derivative is f'(x) = nx^(n-1), derived from the definition using algebraic manipulation.
hint: Apply the difference quotient to (x+h)^n, expand using binomial theorem, and cancel the h in the denominator.

description: The derivative of any constant function f(x) = c is zero because there is no change in output.
hint: The difference quotient (c-c)/h equals zero, so the limit is zero regardless of x.

description: The derivative of a constant function is zero: if f(x) = c, then f'(x) = 0.
hint: Remember that constants don't change, so their rate of change is always zero.

description: The derivative of x^n is nx^(n-1): d/dx[x^n] = nx^(n-1) for any real number n.
hint: Bring the exponent down as a coefficient, then subtract 1 from the exponent.

description: The derivative of a constant times a function equals the constant times the derivative: d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x).
hint: Factor out the constant first, then differentiate what remains.

description: The derivative of a sum equals the sum of derivatives: d/dx[f(x) + g(x)] = f'(x) + g'(x).
hint: Differentiate each term separately and add the results together.

description: The derivative of a difference equals the difference of derivatives: d/dx[f(x) - g(x)] = f'(x) - g'(x).
hint: Differentiate each term separately and subtract the results.

description: The derivative of a product is d/dx[f(x)g(x)] = f'(x)g(x) + f(x)g'(x), not the product of derivatives.
hint: Use the pattern: first times derivative of second, plus second times derivative of first.

description: The derivative of a quotient is d/dx[f(x)/g(x)] = [g(x)f'(x) - f(x)g'(x)]/[g(x)]^2.
hint: Remember 'low d-high minus high d-low, over low squared' as a mnemonic.

description: Complex functions require applying multiple differentiation rules in sequence or simultaneously.
hint: Break the problem into parts, identify which rule applies to each part, then combine results.

description: The derivative of e^x is e^x itself, and d/dx[a^x] = a^x·ln(a) for constant base a.
hint: The natural exponential e^x is its own derivative; other bases need a ln(a) factor.

description: For position function s(t), velocity is v(t)=s'(t) and acceleration is a(t)=v'(t)=s''(t).
hint: Take one derivative to get velocity, take another derivative of velocity to get acceleration.

description: The marginal cost C'(x) approximates the cost of producing one additional unit when x units are already produced.
hint: The derivative of the cost function tells you how much one more item will cost to produce.

description: The derivative P'(t) of a population function P(t) gives the instantaneous rate at which the population is changing at time t.
hint: A positive derivative means population is growing; negative means declining; find magnitude for growth rate.

description: When two quantities are related by an equation and both change with time, their derivatives are related through the chain rule.
hint: Differentiate the entire equation with respect to time, treating all variables as functions of time.

description: Speed is the absolute value of velocity: speed = |v(t)|, so speed is always non-negative while velocity can be negative.
hint: Velocity includes direction (sign matters); speed is just how fast, regardless of direction.

description: A positive derivative indicates the function is increasing, negative indicates decreasing, and zero indicates a possible local extremum or inflection point.
hint: Check the sign of f'(a) to determine if f is going up or down at x=a.

description: The units of f'(x) are the units of f divided by the units of x, reflecting rate of change interpretation.
hint: If position is in meters and time in seconds, velocity has units meters/second.

description: The derivative of sin(x) equals cos(x), proven using the limit definition and trigonometric identities.
hint: Use the limit definition with sin(x+h) expanded via angle sum formula, then apply the special limits of sin(h)/h and (cos(h)-1)/h as h approaches zero.

description: The derivative of cos(x) equals -sin(x), proven similarly to sine using limit definition.
hint: Expand cos(x+h) using the angle sum formula, then factor and apply the same special trigonometric limits used for sine.

description: The fundamental limits lim(h→0) sin(h)/h = 1 and lim(h→0) (cos(h)-1)/h = 0 enable derivative proofs.
hint: Memorize these two limits as they are essential for proving all trigonometric derivatives; use the squeeze theorem or geometric arguments to understand why they hold.

description: The derivative of tan(x) equals sec²(x), derived using the quotient rule on sin(x)/cos(x).
hint: Write tan(x) as sin(x)/cos(x) and apply the quotient rule, then simplify using the Pythagorean identity sin²(x) + cos²(x) = 1.

description: The derivative of sec(x) equals sec(x)tan(x), found by differentiating 1/cos(x) using the quotient rule.
hint: Express sec(x) as 1/cos(x), apply the quotient rule with constant numerator, then rewrite in terms of secant and tangent.

description: The derivative of cot(x) equals -csc²(x), obtained from differentiating cos(x)/sin(x).
hint: Write cot(x) as cos(x)/sin(x) and apply the quotient rule, simplifying the result using trigonometric identities.

description: The derivative of csc(x) equals -csc(x)cot(x), derived from differentiating 1/sin(x).
hint: Express csc(x) as 1/sin(x) and use the quotient rule, then convert to cosecant and cotangent form.

description: Successive derivatives of trigonometric functions follow cyclic patterns, particularly useful for sine and cosine.
hint: Notice that differentiating sin(x) four times returns to sin(x); use this pattern to find any order derivative quickly.

description: A function formed by applying one function to the result of another, written as f(g(x)) or (f ∘ g)(x).
hint: Identify the 'outer' function f and the 'inner' function g separately before attempting differentiation.

description: The derivative of a composite function f(g(x)) equals f'(g(x)) · g'(x), the derivative of the outer function evaluated at the inner function times the derivative of the inner function.
hint: Write out the outer function's derivative first, then multiply by the inner function's derivative as a separate factor.

description: The process of decomposing a composite function into its constituent parts to apply the chain rule correctly.
hint: Ask yourself: what operation happens last when evaluating the function? That's your outer function.

description: Applying the chain rule when the outer function is a power, giving d/dx[u(x)]^n = n[u(x)]^(n-1) · u'(x).
hint: Bring down the exponent, reduce it by one, keep the base unchanged, then multiply by the derivative of the base.

description: Applying the chain rule when the outer or inner function involves trigonometric expressions like sin(u(x)) or cos(u(x)).
hint: Remember the basic trig derivatives, then multiply by the derivative of whatever is inside the trig function.

description: Extending the chain rule to functions with three or more nested compositions, requiring repeated application.
hint: Work from the outermost function inward, applying the chain rule at each layer and multiplying all derivative factors together.

id: Chain Rule Combined with Other Rules
description: Using the chain rule together with product rule, quotient rule, or sum rule in a single differentiation problem.
hint: First determine which rule applies to the overall structure, then use the chain rule on any composite parts within.

description: Expressing the chain rule as dy/dx = (dy/du)(du/dx), emphasizing the cancellation of differentials.
hint: The 'du' appears to cancel like fractions, which can help you remember the correct order of multiplication.

description: If f is differentiable at a and f'(a) ≠ 0, then the inverse function g is differentiable at f(a) and g'(f(a)) = 1/f'(a).
hint: The derivative of the inverse is the reciprocal of the derivative of the original function, evaluated at the corresponding point.

description: Functions of the form f(x) = x^(p/q) where p and q are integers can be differentiated using the power rule extended to rational exponents.
hint: Apply the power rule: bring down the exponent p/q and reduce the exponent by 1.

description: The derivative of arcsin(x) is 1/√(1-x²) for -1 < x < 1.
hint: Use the inverse function theorem with sin(y) = x, remembering that cos²(y) + sin²(y) = 1.

description: The derivative of arccos(x) is -1/√(1-x²) for -1 < x < 1.
hint: Similar to arcsin but with a negative sign because cosine is decreasing on [0, π].

description: The derivative of arctan(x) is 1/(1+x²) for all real x.
hint: Use the inverse function theorem with tan(y) = x and the identity sec²(y) = 1 + tan²(y).

id: Chain Rule with Inverse Trigonometric Functions
description: When differentiating compositions like arcsin(u(x)), apply the chain rule: multiply the derivative of arcsin by u'(x).
hint: Find the derivative of the outer inverse trig function, then multiply by the derivative of the inside function.

description: An explicit function has y isolated (y = f(x)), while an implicit function has x and y mixed together in an equation (e.g., x² + y² = 25).
hint: Check if you can write y = something with only x on the right side; if not, it's implicit.

description: The method of differentiating both sides of an equation with respect to x while treating y as an implicit function of x.
hint: Differentiate term-by-term on both sides, remembering that every y term needs the chain rule with dy/dx.

description: When differentiating terms containing y with respect to x, multiply by dy/dx using the chain rule since y depends on x.
hint: For any function of y like y², use the chain rule: d/dx[y²] = 2y·(dy/dx).

description: After differentiating implicitly, collect all dy/dx terms on one side and factor them out to solve for dy/dx.
hint: Move all terms with dy/dx to the left, all other terms to the right, then factor out dy/dx and divide.

description: When differentiating products like xy implicitly, use the product rule and apply dy/dx to y terms.
hint: For xy, use product rule: d/dx[xy] = x(dy/dx) + y(1) = x(dy/dx) + y.

description: Finding the slope of a tangent line to an implicitly defined curve by evaluating dy/dx at a specific point.
hint: First find dy/dx generally, then substitute both x and y coordinates of the point into your expression.

description: Finding d²y/dx² by implicitly differentiating the expression for dy/dx, treating it as an implicit function.
hint: After finding dy/dx, differentiate it implicitly with respect to x, then substitute your original dy/dx expression.

description: Equations relating x and y that may not define y as a function of x globally but can be differentiated locally.
hint: Even if the equation fails the vertical line test, you can still find slopes at specific points using implicit differentiation.

description: The derivative of e^x equals itself: d/dx(e^x) = e^x.
hint: This is the unique function that is its own derivative; memorize this fundamental property.
section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: For any positive constant a, d/dx(a^x) = a^x ln(a).
hint: The natural log of the base appears as a constant factor; when a=e, ln(e)=1 giving the e^x case.
section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: The derivative of ln(x) is 1/x for x > 0.
hint: Remember the domain restriction; this formula only works for positive x values.
section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: For positive constant a ≠ 1, d/dx(log_a(x)) = 1/(x ln(a)).
hint: Convert to natural logarithm using the change of base formula: log_a(x) = ln(x)/ln(a).
section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: For composite exponentials like e^(g(x)), use chain rule: d/dx(e^(g(x))) = e^(g(x)) · g'(x).
hint: First take the derivative of the outer exponential, then multiply by the derivative of the exponent.
section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: For composite logarithms like ln(g(x)), use chain rule: d/dx(ln(g(x))) = g'(x)/g(x).
hint: The derivative is the inner function's derivative divided by the inner function itself.
section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: Taking ln of both sides before differentiating simplifies products, quotients, and variable exponents.
hint: Use when you have complicated products or functions like x^x; take ln first, then differentiate implicitly.
section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

id: Derivative of Variable Base and Exponent
description: For f(x)^(g(x)), rewrite as e^(g(x)ln(f(x))) then differentiate using chain rule.
hint: When both base and exponent contain x, use the exponential-logarithm identity before differentiating.
section: 3.9 Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: A problem where multiple quantities change over time and are connected by an equation, requiring finding one rate given others.
hint: Identify all changing quantities, write an equation relating them, then differentiate both sides with respect to time.

description: Differentiating both sides of an equation with respect to time t, treating variables as functions of t.
hint: Use the chain rule on every variable that changes with time, writing dx/dt, dy/dt, etc.

description: A systematic five-step approach: sketch, identify variables, write relating equation, differentiate with respect to time, substitute known values.
hint: Always differentiate the equation before substituting numerical values to avoid losing variable relationships.

description: Related rates problems involving changing geometric quantities like radius, volume, area, or side lengths of shapes.
hint: Draw a clear diagram labeling all changing quantities, then use geometric formulas like V = (4/3)πr³ or A = πr².

description: Related rates problems using the Pythagorean theorem to relate changing distances or positions.
hint: If objects move at right angles or distances form a right triangle, write x² + y² = z² and differentiate.

description: Using proportional relationships from similar triangles to eliminate variables in related rates problems.
hint: If triangles remain similar as they change, write a proportion to express one variable in terms of another before differentiating.

description: Determining whether rates are positive or negative based on whether quantities are increasing or decreasing.
hint: If a quantity is decreasing, its rate of change is negative; use the correct sign when substituting known rates.

description: Ensuring all rates and measurements use compatible units (e.g., meters and meters per second, not mixed with centimeters).
hint: Convert all given quantities to consistent units before starting calculations to avoid errors.

description: The tangent line at point (a, f(a)) approximates function values near x = a using L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a).
hint: Start by finding f(a) and f'(a) at your point, then plug into the linearization formula.

description: The linear function L(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a) that best approximates f(x) near x = a.
hint: Evaluate both the function and its derivative at the base point a before constructing L(x).

description: The difference |f(x) - L(x)| between actual function values and linear approximation increases as x moves away from a.
hint: Check how far x is from your base point a; closer means better approximation.

description: The differential dy = f'(x)dx represents the change in the tangent line when the independent variable changes by dx.
hint: Think of dy as the rise along the tangent line, while dx is the horizontal change.

description: The differential dx represents an arbitrary small change in the independent variable x and equals Δx.
hint: Choose dx to match your actual change in x, then compute dy using the derivative.

description: The differential dy approximates the actual change Δy = f(x + dx) - f(x) in function values.
hint: Calculate dy = f'(x)dx first, then compare to Δy by evaluating f at both points.

description: Using differentials to estimate how measurement errors in input values affect calculated output values.
hint: Identify the measurement error as dx, then use dy = f'(x)dx to find the propagated error.

description: Relative error is dy/y and percent error is (dy/y) × 100%, measuring error as a fraction of the quantity.
hint: Divide your differential error by the actual function value to get relative error.

description: A function has an absolute maximum at c if f(c) ≥ f(x) for all x in the domain, and absolute minimum if f(c) ≤ f(x) for all x.
hint: Compare the function value at c to ALL other points in the domain, not just nearby points.

description: A function has a local maximum at c if f(c) ≥ f(x) for all x near c, and local minimum if f(c) ≤ f(x) for all x near c.
hint: Local extrema only compare to nearby points within some small interval around c.

description: A critical point occurs where f'(c) = 0 or f'(c) does not exist, and c is in the domain of f.
hint: Find where the derivative equals zero OR where the derivative is undefined but the function is defined.

description: If f has a local extremum at c and f is differentiable at c, then f'(c) = 0.
hint: Every local max or min where the derivative exists must be a critical point, but not every critical point is an extremum.

description: A continuous function on a closed interval [a,b] must attain both an absolute maximum and absolute minimum on that interval.
hint: Check endpoints and critical points in [a,b] to find where the absolute extrema occur.

description: To find absolute extrema on [a,b]: evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, then compare values to identify max and min.
hint: Make a list of all candidates (critical points plus endpoints), evaluate f at each, then pick the largest and smallest values.

description: When finding extrema on closed intervals, always evaluate the function at the boundary points a and b in addition to interior critical points.
hint: Absolute extrema on closed intervals can occur at endpoints even when the derivative is nonzero there.

description: If f is continuous on [a,b], differentiable on (a,b), and f(a)=f(b), then there exists c in (a,b) where f'(c)=0.
hint: Check all three conditions: continuity on closed interval, differentiability on open interval, and equal endpoint values.

description: If f is continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then there exists c in (a,b) where f'(c) equals the average rate of change (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a).
hint: The instantaneous rate at some point c equals the average rate over the entire interval—draw the secant line and look for a parallel tangent.

description: The process of checking continuity and differentiability conditions before applying the Mean Value Theorem.
hint: Explicitly verify continuity on the closed interval and differentiability on the open interval before concluding MVT applies.

description: Solving the equation f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a) for the specific value(s) of c guaranteed by the Mean Value Theorem.
hint: Set the derivative equal to the slope of the secant line, then solve for c and verify it lies in the open interval (a,b).

description: If f'(x)=0 for all x in an interval, then f is constant on that interval.
hint: Apply MVT to any two points: if f'(c)=0 always, then (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)=0, so f(b)=f(a) for all pairs.

id: Corollary: Same Derivative Implies Differ by Constant
description: If f'(x)=g'(x) for all x in an interval, then f(x)=g(x)+C for some constant C.
hint: Consider the function h(x)=f(x)-g(x) and show its derivative is zero everywhere.

description: The Mean Value Theorem guarantees at least one point where the tangent line is parallel to the secant line connecting the endpoints.
hint: Visualize the secant line from (a,f(a)) to (b,f(b)); MVT says there's a point where the tangent has this same slope.

description: Using the Mean Value Theorem to establish bounds or inequalities involving function values based on derivative constraints.
hint: If you know bounds on f'(x), use MVT to write f(b)-f(a)=f'(c)(b-a) and substitute the bounds.

description: If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, then f is increasing there; if f'(x) < 0, then f is decreasing there.
hint: Find where the derivative is positive or negative by testing values in intervals between critical points.
section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

id: First Derivative Test for Local Extrema
description: At a critical point c, if f' changes from positive to negative, f has a local maximum; if f' changes from negative to positive, f has a local minimum.
hint: Check the sign of f' on both sides of the critical point to see if the function changes from increasing to decreasing or vice versa.
section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

description: If f''(x) > 0 on an interval, f is concave up there; if f''(x) < 0, f is concave down there.
hint: Calculate the second derivative and determine its sign on intervals between inflection point candidates.
section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

description: A point where the concavity of f changes from up to down or down to up.
hint: Find where f'' changes sign, and verify that f is continuous at that point.
section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

id: Second Derivative Test for Local Extrema
description: At a critical point c where f'(c) = 0, if f''(c) > 0 then f has a local minimum; if f''(c) < 0 then f has a local maximum.
hint: After finding critical points with f'(c) = 0, evaluate f''(c) to quickly classify the extremum without checking sign changes.
section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

description: A number line showing where a derivative is positive, negative, or zero to analyze function behavior.
hint: Mark critical points and discontinuities on a number line, then test the derivative's sign in each resulting interval.
section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

description: A systematic approach using derivatives to sketch graphs by finding critical points, intervals of increase/decrease, concavity, and inflection points.
hint: Work through steps: find domain, intercepts, asymptotes, critical points, f' analysis, f'' analysis, then sketch.
section: 4.5 Derivatives and the Shape of a Graph

description: The value L that f(x) approaches as x approaches positive or negative infinity, written as lim(x→∞) f(x) = L or lim(x→-∞) f(x) = L.
hint: Evaluate what happens to f(x) as x gets arbitrarily large (positive or negative) by analyzing dominant terms.

description: A horizontal line y = L that the graph of f(x) approaches as x approaches positive or negative infinity.
hint: Find limits at infinity; if they exist and equal L, then y = L is a horizontal asymptote.

description: The behavior of rational functions as x approaches infinity, determined by comparing degrees of numerator and denominator.
hint: Compare degrees: if numerator degree equals denominator degree, divide leading coefficients for the horizontal asymptote.

description: When f(x) approaches positive or negative infinity as x approaches a finite value a, written as lim(x→a) f(x) = ±∞.
hint: Check if denominator approaches zero while numerator remains nonzero; determine sign from one-sided limits.

description: A vertical line x = a where f(x) approaches positive or negative infinity as x approaches a from one or both sides.
hint: Find values where denominator equals zero but numerator doesn't; verify the function is unbounded there.

description: Extended limit laws that apply when x approaches infinity, including properties for sums, products, and quotients of limits.
hint: Apply standard limit laws treating infinity as the limit point; divide by highest power of x in rational expressions.

description: Exponential functions e^x grow faster than any polynomial as x→∞, while e^(-x) approaches zero.
hint: Remember e^x dominates polynomials for large x; e^(-x) decays to zero faster than any polynomial grows.

description: Expressions like ∞/∞ or ∞-∞ that require algebraic manipulation or L'Hôpital's Rule to evaluate limits at infinity.
hint: For ∞/∞ forms in rational functions, divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of x present.

description: Process of identifying the quantity to optimize and the constraints from a word problem.
hint: Start by clearly labeling what you want to maximize or minimize, then list all given constraints.

description: The objective function (to optimize) and constraint equations (relating variables) in an optimization problem.
hint: Write one equation for what you're optimizing and separate equations for geometric or physical constraints.

description: Using constraint equations to express the objective function in terms of one independent variable.
hint: Solve a constraint equation for one variable and substitute into your objective function to eliminate it.

description: Determining the valid interval for the independent variable based on real-world limitations.
hint: Consider what values make physical sense: lengths must be positive, angles have bounds, etc.

description: Finding optimal values by setting the derivative equal to zero and testing critical points.
hint: After finding critical points, check endpoints too if your domain is a closed interval.

description: Comparing function values at critical points and interval endpoints to find the absolute extremum.
hint: Always evaluate your function at boundary values of the domain, not just where the derivative is zero.

description: Applications involving maximizing or minimizing area, volume, perimeter, or distance using geometric relationships.
hint: Draw a diagram and label all quantities; use formulas like area = length × width or volume = πr²h.

description: Applications involving cost, revenue, or profit functions where marginal analysis uses derivatives.
hint: Revenue = price × quantity; Profit = Revenue - Cost; maximize profit by setting its derivative to zero.

description: A limit that approaches 0/0, requiring further analysis beyond direct substitution.
hint: Check if both numerator and denominator approach zero independently at the limit point.

description: A limit that approaches ∞/∞, requiring techniques beyond basic limit laws.
hint: Verify both numerator and denominator grow without bound in the same direction.

description: If lim f(x)/g(x) gives 0/0, then it equals lim f'(x)/g'(x) if conditions are met.
hint: Differentiate numerator and denominator separately, not as a quotient rule.

description: If lim f(x)/g(x) gives ∞/∞, then it equals lim f'(x)/g'(x) if conditions are met.
hint: Apply the rule repeatedly if needed, checking for indeterminate form each time.

description: A limit of the form 0·∞ that requires rewriting as a quotient before applying L'Hôpital's Rule.
hint: Convert to 0/0 or ∞/∞ by rewriting as a fraction with one factor in the denominator.

description: A limit of the form ∞−∞ requiring algebraic manipulation to create a quotient form.
hint: Find a common denominator or use conjugate multiplication to eliminate the difference.

description: Indeterminate forms involving exponents, solved using logarithms to convert to quotients.
hint: Take the natural logarithm of the expression first, then apply L'Hôpital's Rule to the exponent.

description: Using L'Hôpital's Rule to compare relative growth rates of functions as x approaches infinity.
hint: Exponentials grow faster than polynomials, which grow faster than logarithms.

description: The formula x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n) that generates successive approximations to a root of f(x)=0.
hint: Start with an initial guess x_0, then repeatedly apply the formula using both the function value and its derivative at each step.

description: Newton's method finds where the tangent line at (x_n, f(x_n)) crosses the x-axis to get the next approximation x_{n+1}.
hint: Draw the tangent line at your current point and see where it hits the x-axis — that's your next guess.

description: Choosing a starting value x_0 close to the actual root to ensure Newton's method converges.
hint: Graph the function or evaluate it at a few points to find where it changes sign, then pick a nearby starting value.

description: Newton's method converges when the initial guess is sufficiently close to a root and f'(x) is nonzero near the root.
hint: Check that your starting point isn't near a horizontal tangent (where f'≈0) or far from the actual root.

description: Situations where Newton's method fails or diverges, including f'(x_n)=0, poor initial guess, or cycling between values.
hint: If your derivative equals zero at some iteration, the method breaks down — try a different starting point.

description: The systematic procedure of repeatedly applying Newton's formula until successive approximations differ by less than a tolerance.
hint: Keep calculating new x values until |x_{n+1} - x_n| is smaller than your desired accuracy threshold.

description: Newton's method typically exhibits quadratic convergence, meaning the number of correct digits approximately doubles with each iteration.
hint: Once you're reasonably close to the root, expect the decimal accuracy to improve dramatically with each step.

description: Using Newton's method to approximate roots of equations arising from real-world problems in physics, engineering, or optimization.
hint: First set up your equation as f(x)=0, compute f'(x), choose a reasonable starting guess, then iterate.

description: A function F is an antiderivative of f if F'(x) = f(x) for all x in the domain.
hint: To verify an antiderivative, take its derivative and check if you get back the original function.

description: The general antiderivative of f is F(x) + C where F is any particular antiderivative and C is an arbitrary constant.
hint: Remember that two functions with the same derivative differ only by a constant, so add +C to every antiderivative.

description: The antiderivative of x^n is (x^(n+1))/(n+1) + C for n ≠ -1.
hint: Increase the exponent by 1, then divide by the new exponent; don't forget +C at the end.

description: The antiderivative of e^x is e^x + C, and the antiderivative of a^x is a^x/ln(a) + C.
hint: For e^x, the antiderivative is itself; for other bases a^x, divide by ln(a).

description: Basic antiderivatives include ∫sin(x)dx = -cos(x) + C and ∫cos(x)dx = sin(x) + C.
hint: Remember sin integrates to negative cosine, and cosine integrates to positive sine.

description: The antiderivative of 1/x is ln|x| + C for x ≠ 0.
hint: This is the special case where the power rule fails (n = -1); use natural logarithm with absolute value.

description: Finding a specific antiderivative by using a given point (x₀, y₀) to determine the constant C.
hint: First find the general antiderivative with +C, then substitute the given point to solve for C.

description: Finding velocity and position functions by antidifferentiating acceleration a(t), using initial conditions.
hint: Antidifferentiate acceleration to get velocity, then antidifferentiate velocity to get position; use initial conditions to find both constants.

description: A sum of rectangular areas that approximates the area under a curve by dividing the region into subintervals.
hint: Start by dividing your interval into equal parts and decide where to sample the height in each subinterval.

description: A Riemann sum using the left endpoint of each subinterval to determine rectangle height.
hint: Use f(x₀), f(x₁), ..., f(xₙ₋₁) as your heights and multiply each by the width Δx.

description: A Riemann sum using the right endpoint of each subinterval to determine rectangle height.
hint: Use f(x₁), f(x₂), ..., f(xₙ) as your heights and multiply each by the width Δx.

description: A Riemann sum using the midpoint of each subinterval to determine rectangle height.
hint: For each subinterval [xᵢ, xᵢ₊₁], evaluate f at (xᵢ + xᵢ₊₁)/2 to get better accuracy than endpoints.

description: The compact notation Σᵢ₌₁ⁿ f(xᵢ)Δx for representing Riemann sums as summations.
hint: Identify the pattern in your sum, then write it as Σ with clear limits and index variable.

description: The process of increasing the number of subintervals to improve area approximation accuracy.
hint: As n increases and Δx decreases, your rectangular approximation becomes more accurate.

description: Riemann sums using maximum and minimum function values on each subinterval to bound the true area.
hint: For upper sum use the highest point in each subinterval; for lower sum use the lowest point.

description: Taking the limit as n→∞ of a Riemann sum to obtain the exact area under a curve.
hint: Set up your Riemann sum formula first, then take lim(n→∞) to transition from approximation to exact value.

description: The definite integral of f from a to b is the limit of Riemann sums as partition size approaches zero, written ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx.
hint: Start with Riemann sums and imagine making the rectangles infinitely thin—the limit is the definite integral.

description: A function is integrable on [a,b] if the limit of Riemann sums exists and is the same for all choices of sample points.
hint: Check if upper and lower sums approach the same value as partition size decreases.

description: Definite integrals satisfy linearity: ∫(cf)dx = c∫f dx, ∫(f±g)dx = ∫f dx ± ∫g dx, and additivity over intervals.
hint: Split complicated integrals using constant multiples and sums, or break the interval into pieces.

description: The definite integral represents signed area: positive where f(x)>0, negative where f(x)<0.
hint: Draw the curve and shade regions above and below the x-axis separately, tracking signs.

description: If f(x)≤g(x) on [a,b], then ∫ₐᵇ f dx ≤ ∫ₐᵇ g dx; useful for estimation.
hint: Find simpler functions that bound your integrand above and below to estimate the integral.

description: The average value of f on [a,b] is (1/(b-a))∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx, representing the constant height with same area.
hint: Divide the definite integral by the width of the interval to find average height.

description: In ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx, x is a dummy variable; ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = ∫ₐᵇ f(t)dt produces the same number.
hint: The variable of integration can be renamed freely—only the function and endpoints matter.

description: For constant c, ∫ₐᵇ c dx = c(b-a), the area of a rectangle.
hint: The integral of a constant is just that constant times the width of the interval.

description: If f is continuous on [a,b], then the function g(x) = ∫[a to x] f(t)dt is differentiable and g'(x) = f(x).
hint: Remember that taking the derivative of an accumulation function returns the original integrand—differentiation undoes integration.

description: A function defined as the integral from a constant to a variable upper limit, A(x) = ∫[a to x] f(t)dt.
hint: Think of this as accumulating area under the curve f as x increases from the starting point a.

description: If F is an antiderivative of f on [a,b], then ∫[a to b] f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a).
hint: To evaluate a definite integral, find any antiderivative F, then compute the difference F(b) - F(a).

description: The definite integral of a rate of change equals the net change in the quantity over the interval.
hint: If F'(x) represents velocity, then ∫[a to b] F'(x)dx gives the displacement from time a to time b.

description: The notation F(x)|[a to b] or [F(x)]^b_a represents F(b) - F(a) for evaluating definite integrals.
hint: Always substitute the upper limit first, then subtract the value at the lower limit.

description: When differentiating ∫[a to g(x)] f(t)dt with variable upper limit g(x), apply the chain rule: d/dx = f(g(x))·g'(x).
hint: First apply FTC Part 1 to get f(g(x)), then multiply by the derivative of the upper limit g'(x).

description: For ∫[h(x) to b] f(t)dt with variable lower limit, rewrite as -∫[b to h(x)] f(t)dt before differentiating.
hint: Use the property that ∫[a to b] = -∫[b to a] to convert variable lower limits to upper limits.

description: Net area accounts for regions below the x-axis as negative, while total area uses absolute value |f(x)| to count all regions positively.
hint: If the function crosses the x-axis, split the integral at zeros and take absolute values of each piece for total area.

description: Collection of standard antiderivative formulas including power, exponential, and trigonometric functions applied to definite integrals.
hint: Apply the Fundamental Theorem Part 2: integrate using antiderivative formulas, then substitute limits.
section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

description: For even functions f(-x)=f(x), the integral from -a to a equals twice the integral from 0 to a.
hint: Check if f(-x) equals f(x), then use symmetry to compute only from 0 to a and double the result.
section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

description: For odd functions f(-x)=-f(x), the integral from -a to a equals zero due to symmetric cancellation.
hint: Verify f(-x)=-f(x) over a symmetric interval; the positive and negative areas cancel exactly.
section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

description: The definite integral of a rate function F'(t) from a to b gives the net change F(b)-F(a) in quantity F.
hint: Identify what quantity is changing, then integrate its rate of change over the time interval.
section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

description: The integral of velocity v(t) over time gives net displacement (final position minus initial position).
hint: Integrate velocity to find displacement; this is net change, not total distance traveled.
section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

description: The integral of |v(t)| gives total distance traveled, accounting for direction changes.
hint: Split the integral at points where velocity changes sign, then integrate absolute values separately.
section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

description: Applications of net change theorem to problems involving mass, population, volume, or other accumulating quantities.
hint: Set up the integral of the rate function with correct units; result has units of the accumulated quantity.
section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

description: Using function symmetry (even/odd) to simplify integrals over symmetric intervals centered at origin.
hint: Before computing, check for symmetry about the y-axis (even) or origin (odd) to reduce calculation work.
section: 5.4 Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem

description: A technique to simplify integrals by substituting u for a function inside a composite function, transforming the integral into a simpler form.
hint: Choose u as the inner function of a composition, then express dx in terms of du using the derivative.

description: The selection of an appropriate substitution variable u to simplify an integral, typically the inner function of a composite or a function whose derivative appears.
hint: Look for a function whose derivative (or a constant multiple) appears elsewhere in the integrand.

description: The differential relationship du = (du/dx)dx used to transform dx into du in substitution, derived from the derivative of u with respect to x.
hint: After choosing u, differentiate it to find du/dx, then solve for dx in terms of du.

description: Applying u-substitution to definite integrals by either changing the limits of integration to u-values or substituting back before evaluating.
hint: When substituting in definite integrals, transform both limits using u = g(x), or revert to x before applying bounds.

description: The process of converting integration bounds from x-values to u-values when performing substitution on definite integrals.
hint: Substitute the original x-limits into your u-function to get the new u-limits.

description: Identifying when an integrand contains a composite function f(g(x)) where the derivative g'(x) also appears, making it suitable for substitution.
hint: Look for patterns like f(g(x))·g'(x) where the derivative of the inner function is present as a factor.

description: The step of replacing u with the original expression in terms of x after integrating with respect to u in indefinite integrals.
hint: After integrating in terms of u, substitute back using your original u = g(x) equation before adding the constant.

description: The technique of multiplying and dividing by constants to match the exact form needed after substitution when du differs from the differential in the integrand by a constant factor.
hint: If du = k·f(x)dx but you have f(x)dx, factor out 1/k outside the integral and use k·f(x)dx = du inside.

description: The antiderivative of e^x is e^x plus a constant, making exponential base e particularly convenient for integration.
hint: Remember that e^x is its own derivative, so it's also its own antiderivative.
section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: The antiderivative of a^x (where a > 0, a ≠ 1) is a^x/ln(a) plus a constant.
hint: Convert a^x to e^(x·ln(a)) first, then use substitution with u = x·ln(a) to derive the formula.
section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: The antiderivative of 1/x is ln|x| plus a constant, where absolute value ensures the domain includes negative x.
hint: Always include absolute value bars: ln|x|, not just ln(x), to handle negative domains.
section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: Using u-substitution when the integrand contains exponential expressions, often where u equals the exponent.
hint: Let u be the exponent in e^(something), then find du and replace all x-terms.
section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: Using u-substitution when the integrand contains logarithmic expressions, often letting u = ln(x) or the argument of ln.
hint: If you see ln(x) in the integrand, try u = ln(x) so du = 1/x dx, which often appears nearby.
section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: Recognizing integrands of the form f'(x)/f(x) which integrate to ln|f(x)| plus a constant.
hint: When you see a fraction with the derivative of the denominator in the numerator, the answer involves ln of the denominator.
section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: Evaluating definite integrals of exponential functions that model growth or decay processes over a time interval.
hint: For ke^(rt), factor out constants first, then integrate e^(rt) using the formula for exponential integrals.
section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: Integrating expressions that multiply exponential functions with polynomial or algebraic terms, requiring substitution or pattern recognition.
hint: Look for whether the algebraic part is the derivative of the exponent, which signals u-substitution.
section: 5.6 Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

description: The integral ∫1/√(a²-x²)dx equals arcsin(x/a) + C, deriving from the derivative of inverse sine.
hint: Recognize the pattern a²-x² under the square root and check if the numerator is constant 1.
section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

description: The integral ∫1/(a²+x²)dx equals (1/a)arctan(x/a) + C, deriving from the derivative of inverse tangent.
hint: Look for a sum of squares in the denominator and factor out a² to match the standard form.
section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

description: The integral ∫1/(x√(x²-a²))dx equals (1/a)arcsec(|x|/a) + C, deriving from the derivative of inverse secant.
hint: Notice the x²-a² pattern under the square root with x in the denominator, indicating arcsecant form.
section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

description: Rewriting quadratic expressions ax²+bx+c in the form a(x-h)²+k to match inverse trigonometric integral patterns.
hint: Complete the square to transform the quadratic into a form matching a²±x² patterns.
section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

id: Algebraic Manipulation for Inverse Trig Forms
description: Factoring constants and rearranging expressions to match standard inverse trigonometric integral forms.
hint: Factor out constants from denominators to isolate a²±x² patterns before integrating.
section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

description: Combining substitution with inverse trigonometric formulas when the integrand involves composite functions.
hint: After substitution, check if the result matches one of the three basic inverse trig integral forms.
section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

id: Domain Restrictions in Inverse Trig Integrals
description: Understanding when integrals yielding inverse trig functions are valid based on domain constraints like |x|<a or |x|>a.
hint: Check that x values satisfy the domain requirements (e.g., a²-x²>0) before applying the formula.
section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

description: Identifying which inverse trigonometric function (arcsin, arctan, or arcsec) applies based on the integrand structure.
hint: Look at whether you have a difference under a square root (arcsin), a sum of squares (arctan), or a product with square root (arcsec).
section: 5.7 Integrals Resulting in Inverse Trigonometric Functions

description: The region bounded by two functions f(x) and g(x) over an interval [a,b] is found by integrating the difference of the functions.
hint: Identify which function is on top and which is on bottom, then integrate (top - bottom)dx from a to b.

description: For vertical strips, the integrand is the upper curve minus the lower curve: ∫[f(x) - g(x)]dx where f(x) ≥ g(x).
hint: Sketch both curves to visually determine which function has larger y-values in your interval.

description: Setting f(x) = g(x) and solving determines where curves meet, providing integration limits or partition points.
hint: Set the two functions equal and solve algebraically or graphically to find x-values where curves cross.

description: When curves cross within [a,b], split the integral into subintervals where one function stays consistently above the other.
hint: Break your integral at each intersection point and reverse which function is on top in each piece.

description: For regions better described by x as a function of y, integrate with respect to y using ∫[g(y) - h(y)]dy where g(y) is rightmost.
hint: If solving for x is easier than solving for y, consider switching to dy and integrating along horizontal strips.

description: A special case where g(x) = 0, giving area = ∫|f(x)|dx to ensure positive area values.
hint: This reduces to finding the definite integral, but remember to account for regions where f(x) is negative.

id: Deciding Between dx and dy Integration
description: Choose integration variable based on which orientation gives simpler function expressions and fewer subintervals.
hint: If vertical lines through the region hit the boundary twice (top and bottom), use dx; if horizontal lines work better, use dy.

id: Setting Up Limits from Region Description
description: Translate geometric descriptions of bounded regions into concrete integration limits a and b (or c and d for dy).
hint: Draw the region, mark all boundary curves, and identify the leftmost/rightmost (or bottom/top) extent of the region.

description: Finding volume by integrating cross-sectional areas perpendicular to an axis: V = ∫[a to b] A(x)dx.
hint: Identify the axis of slicing, find the area formula A(x) at each slice, then integrate over the interval.

description: The area of a slice perpendicular to the axis at position x, which varies with x.
hint: Express the cross-section dimensions in terms of x using the boundary curves or shapes.

description: Finding volume when a region revolves around an axis using circular cross-sections: V = π∫[a to b] [R(x)]²dx.
hint: The radius R(x) is the distance from the axis of rotation to the curve; square it and multiply by π.

description: Finding volume when a region between two curves revolves around an axis: V = π∫[a to b] ([R(x)]² - [r(x)]²)dx.
hint: Identify outer radius R(x) and inner radius r(x) from the axis; subtract their squares inside the integral.

description: Volume calculation when rotating about y = k, requiring vertical distances as radii.
hint: Express radii as |curve - k| to measure vertical distance from the axis of rotation.

description: Volume calculation when rotating about x = k, often requiring dy integration with functions x(y).
hint: Rewrite curves as x in terms of y, then integrate with respect to dy using horizontal distances.

description: Computing volume when slices are squares, triangles, or other shapes with area formulas in terms of base dimensions.
hint: Find the base dimension from the region boundaries, apply the shape's area formula, then integrate.

description: Deciding whether to integrate with dx or dy based on slice orientation and problem setup.
hint: Slice perpendicular to your integration variable: vertical slices use dx, horizontal slices use dy.

description: A technique for finding volumes of revolution by integrating the surface areas of nested cylindrical shells.
hint: Picture peeling an onion layer by layer—each shell has radius (distance from axis), height (function value), and thickness (dx or dy).

description: The radius is the perpendicular distance from the rotation axis to the shell, and the height is the length of the shell parallel to the axis.
hint: If rotating about the y-axis using dx, radius is x and height is f(x); sketch a single shell to see these dimensions.

description: The volume of a thin cylindrical shell is approximated by 2πrh·thickness, where r is radius and h is height.
hint: Think of 'unrolling' the shell into a thin rectangular slab with dimensions 2πr (circumference) by h by thickness.

description: The integral for shell method is ∫2π(radius)(height)dx or dy, where radius and height are functions of the integration variable.
hint: Identify which variable is parallel to the axis of rotation—that determines whether you use dx or dy.

description: Shell method is often simpler when slicing parallel to the rotation axis, while washer method slices perpendicular to it.
hint: If washer method requires splitting into multiple integrals or solving for x, try shell method instead.

description: When rotating about lines like x=a or y=b, the shell radius becomes |variable - a| or |variable - b|.
hint: The radius is always the perpendicular distance from the shell to the axis of rotation, not to the origin.

description: For rotation about the x-axis using shells, integrate with respect to y, where radius is y and height is expressed as x(y).
hint: When the axis of rotation is horizontal, your shells are horizontal rings, so use dy and express everything in terms of y.

description: When a region is bounded by multiple curves, the shell height is the difference between the rightmost and leftmost (or top and bottom) boundaries.
hint: Shell height works like 'top minus bottom' in area problems—it's the extent of the region at a given radius.

description: The arc length of a curve y=f(x) from x=a to x=b is given by L = ∫[a,b] √(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx.
hint: Start by finding the derivative f'(x), then square it, add 1, take the square root, and integrate.
section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

description: The infinitesimal arc length element ds is given by ds = √(1 + (dy/dx)²) dx, representing a tiny segment of the curve.
hint: Think of ds as the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs dx and dy using the Pythagorean theorem.
section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

id: Arc Length for x as Function of y
description: When x is expressed as a function of y, the arc length is L = ∫[c,d] √(1 + [dx/dy]²) dy.
hint: If solving for x in terms of y is easier than y in terms of x, use this form with dx/dy instead.
section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

id: Surface Area of Revolution About x-axis
description: The surface area when rotating y=f(x) about the x-axis from x=a to x=b is S = ∫[a,b] 2πf(x)√(1 + [f'(x)]²) dx.
hint: The formula combines the circumference 2πr (where r=f(x)) with the arc length element.
section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

id: Surface Area of Revolution About y-axis
description: The surface area when rotating x=g(y) about the y-axis from y=c to y=d is S = ∫[c,d] 2πg(y)√(1 + [g'(y)]²) dy.
hint: Use radius r=g(y) for rotation about the y-axis, similar to the x-axis case but with roles switched.
section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

description: Techniques for simplifying √(1 + [f'(x)]²) include algebraic manipulation, trigonometric identities, and recognizing perfect squares.
hint: Look for perfect square patterns under the radical that might simplify before integrating.
section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

description: When arc length integrals cannot be evaluated analytically, numerical methods like Simpson's rule or technology must be used.
hint: If the integrand with the square root looks intractable, set up the integral correctly and use a calculator or computer.
section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

description: The differential surface area element dS = 2πr·ds where r is the distance from the axis of rotation and ds is the arc length element.
hint: Surface area combines revolution (2πr) with the stretched length of the curve (ds), not just dx or dy.
section: 6.4 Arc Length of a Curve and Surface Area

description: Work is the integral of force over displacement when force varies with position.
hint: Set up the integral as W = ∫F(x)dx where F(x) is the force function and integrate over the displacement interval.

description: Work to compress or stretch a spring uses F(x) = kx where k is the spring constant.
hint: Remember the natural length is where force is zero; integrate kx from initial to final displacement.

description: Work to lift an object involves integrating weight times distance, accounting for variable mass or position.
hint: If mass varies with height (like a rope or chain), express mass as a function of position before integrating.

description: Work to pump liquid from a tank integrates the weight of each slice times the distance it travels.
hint: Slice horizontally, find volume of each slice, multiply by weight density and lifting distance, then integrate.

description: Pressure increases with depth, so force on a submerged plate is the integral of pressure times area over depth.
hint: Use pressure P = ρgh at depth h, slice the surface horizontally, and integrate ρgh·width(h)·dh.

description: Total mass is the integral of density function over the region when density varies with position.
hint: Express density as ρ(x) and integrate over the length, area, or volume of the object.

description: The center of mass coordinates are found by integrating position times density and dividing by total mass.
hint: For a rod, use x̄ = (1/m)∫x·ρ(x)dx where m is total mass; set up separate integrals for numerator and denominator.

description: Moment is the integral of distance from axis times the mass element, measuring rotational tendency.
hint: Identify the axis, express perpendicular distance as a function, then integrate distance times dm.

description: The moment of a point mass is the product of mass and perpendicular distance from an axis.
hint: Multiply the mass by its distance from the axis you're taking the moment about.

description: The total moment of a system is the sum of individual moments of all point masses.
hint: Add up all the individual mass×distance products for each point in your system.

id: Center of Mass of Point Masses
description: The center of mass is located at x̄ = M_y/m and ȳ = M_x/m, where m is total mass and M are moments.
hint: Divide the moment about each axis by the total mass to find the coordinate.

description: For a thin plate (lamina), M_x = ∫∫ y·ρ(x,y) dA, where ρ is density per unit area.
hint: The y-coordinate of each infinitesimal mass element gets multiplied by its mass, then integrated.

description: For a thin plate, M_y = ∫∫ x·ρ(x,y) dA, measuring tendency to rotate about the y-axis.
hint: The x-coordinate of each infinitesimal mass element gets multiplied by its mass, then integrated.
