description: The centroid is the center of mass when density is constant (uniform), depending only on geometry.
hint: For uniform density, factor it out and the centroid depends only on area and first moments of area.

description: With vertical strips, x̄ = (1/A)∫x·[f(x)-g(x)]dx and ȳ = (1/A)∫½[f(x)²-g(x)²]dx.
hint: The midpoint of each strip is at y = ½[f(x)+g(x)], and its height provides the weight.

description: The volume of a solid of revolution equals the area of the region times the distance traveled by its centroid.
hint: Calculate V = 2πr̄A where r̄ is the distance from centroid to axis of rotation and A is area.

description: If a region is symmetric about a line, its center of mass lies on that line of symmetry.
hint: Before computing integrals, check for symmetry to reduce calculations by setting one coordinate immediately.

description: Integrals of the form ∫e^(kx)dx equal (1/k)e^(kx) + C for constant k.
hint: Factor out the constant from the exponent, integrate e^u, then divide by the coefficient of x.

description: The integral ∫(1/x)dx equals ln|x| + C, the fundamental logarithmic antiderivative.
hint: Remember the absolute value in ln|x| to handle negative x values in the domain.

description: Integrals of the form ∫1/(ax+b)dx equal (1/a)ln|ax+b| + C using substitution.
hint: Let u = ax+b, then du = a dx, and factor out 1/a before integrating 1/u.

description: Transforming exponential integrals using u-substitution when the exponent is a composite function.
hint: Identify the inner function as u, compute du, and verify all terms convert properly before integrating.

description: The integral ∫a^x dx equals a^x/ln(a) + C for any positive base a ≠ 1.
hint: Rewrite a^x as e^(x ln a) first, then use the exponential rule with k = ln(a).

description: Computing ∫ln(x)dx requires integration by parts with u = ln(x) and dv = dx.
hint: Set u = ln(x) so du = (1/x)dx, and let dv = dx so v = x, then apply the integration by parts formula.

description: Finding areas bounded by exponential functions y = e^(kx) over an interval [a,b].
hint: Set up the definite integral with exponential growth formula and evaluate at the bounds.

description: Applying volume methods when the radius or height function involves logarithms.
hint: Express the volume element using ln(x) in the appropriate formula, then integrate carefully with proper bounds.

description: A model where the rate of change of a quantity is proportional to its current value, expressed as dy/dt = ky where k > 0.
hint: Set up the differential equation by recognizing that 'rate proportional to amount' means dy/dt equals k times y.

description: A model where the rate of change of a quantity is negatively proportional to its current value, expressed as dy/dt = -ky where k > 0.
hint: Use negative k for decay; the solution will have e raised to a negative exponent causing the function to decrease.

description: The technique of rewriting dy/dt = ky as dy/y = k dt to solve the differential equation by integration.
hint: Move all y terms to one side with dy and all t terms to the other side with dt, then integrate both sides.

description: The general solution to dy/dt = ky, where C is determined by initial conditions and represents y(0).
hint: After separating and integrating, exponentiate both sides to solve for y; use y(0) to find C.

description: The time required for a decaying quantity to reduce to half its initial value, related to decay constant by t_half = ln(2)/k.
hint: Set y(t_half) equal to half the initial amount and solve for k using the exponential decay formula.

description: The time required for a growing quantity to double its initial value, related to growth constant by t_double = ln(2)/k.
hint: Set y(t_double) equal to twice the initial amount and solve for k using the exponential growth formula.

id: Initial Value Problems for Exponential Models
description: Problems requiring determination of the constant C in y = Ce^(kt) using a condition y(t_0) = y_0.
hint: Substitute the given initial time and value into y = Ce^(kt) and solve for C before finding other unknowns.

description: A model stating that the rate of temperature change is proportional to the difference between object temperature and ambient temperature: dT/dt = -k(T - T_ambient).
hint: Let u = T - T_ambient to transform this into a standard exponential decay equation for u.

description: sinh(x) = (e^x - e^(-x))/2 and cosh(x) = (e^x + e^(-x))/2 define the basic hyperbolic functions.
hint: Write sinh and cosh in terms of exponentials first, then use exponential differentiation rules.

description: d/dx[sinh(x)] = cosh(x) and d/dx[cosh(x)] = sinh(x) are the fundamental derivative formulas.
hint: Notice the pattern mirrors trig derivatives but without sign changes for cosh.

description: tanh(x) = sinh(x)/cosh(x), and similarly sech, csch, coth complete the hyperbolic function family.
hint: Use quotient rule to find derivatives after expressing in terms of sinh and cosh.

description: The fundamental hyperbolic identity relating cosh and sinh, analogous to cos²x + sin²x = 1.
hint: Derive this by squaring the exponential definitions and simplifying.

description: ∫sinh(x)dx = cosh(x) + C and ∫cosh(x)dx = sinh(x) + C are the basic antiderivatives.
hint: These are just the reverse of the derivative formulas you already know.

description: sinh⁻¹(x), cosh⁻¹(x), and tanh⁻¹(x) are defined as inverses of hyperbolic functions with appropriate domains.
hint: Express inverse hyperbolic functions as natural logarithms using the exponential definitions.

description: d/dx[sinh⁻¹(x)] = 1/√(x²+1) and similar formulas involve algebraic expressions with radicals.
hint: Use implicit differentiation on y = sinh(x) where x = sinh⁻¹(y), then swap variables.

description: The curve y = a·cosh(x/a) describes a hanging chain or cable under its own weight.
hint: The catenary minimizes potential energy and naturally involves cosh due to the shape's differential equation.

description: The formula ∫u dv = uv - ∫v du, derived from the product rule for differentiation.
hint: Identify which part of the integrand to call u (usually the part that simplifies when differentiated) and which to call dv (the part you can integrate).

description: The selection process for u and dv using priority rules like LIATE (Logarithmic, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential).
hint: Use LIATE as a guide: choose u as the function that appears first in this list, and let dv be the rest.

description: Applying integration by parts multiple times when a single application doesn't yield an elementary integral.
hint: After the first application, treat the new integral the same way, keeping track of your u and dv choices consistently.

description: Using integration by parts when the integrand contains a polynomial factor multiplied by an exponential or trigonometric function.
hint: Let u be the polynomial (it simplifies when differentiated) and dv be the exponential or trigonometric part.

description: A systematic tabular approach for repeated integration by parts, especially efficient for polynomial times exponential or trigonometric functions.
hint: Create columns for derivatives of u and integrals of dv, alternating signs, then combine diagonally to get your answer.

description: Applying integration by parts to integrals involving logarithmic functions, typically setting u = ln(x).
hint: When you see ln(x) alone or multiplied by a polynomial, let u = ln(x) since its derivative 1/x is simpler.

id: Integration by Parts with Inverse Trigonometric Functions
description: Using integration by parts for integrals containing inverse trigonometric functions like arctan(x) or arcsin(x).
hint: Let u equal the inverse trig function because its derivative is algebraic and often simpler to work with.

description: A technique where repeated integration by parts eventually returns to the original integral, allowing algebraic solution.
hint: If after two applications you see the original integral reappear, move it to the left side and solve algebraically.

description: Integrals of the form ∫sin^m(x)cos^n(x)dx where m and n are positive integers.
hint: If one exponent is odd, strip off one factor and use the Pythagorean identity to convert the rest; if both are even, use power-reducing formulas.

description: Using sin²x + cos²x = 1 to convert between sine and cosine powers during integration.
hint: When you have an odd power, save one factor for du and convert the remaining even power using the identity.

description: Identities like sin²x = (1-cos(2x))/2 and cos²x = (1+cos(2x))/2 used to integrate even powers.
hint: Apply these formulas when both sine and cosine have even powers to convert to first powers of double angles.

description: Integrals of the form ∫tan^m(x)sec^n(x)dx requiring strategic use of trigonometric identities.
hint: If secant has even power, strip off sec²x for du and convert remaining secants using 1+tan²x = sec²x; if tangent is odd, strip off sec(x)tan(x).

description: Using 1 + tan²x = sec²x to convert between tangent and secant powers during integration.
hint: This identity lets you express secants in terms of tangents or vice versa when setting up u-substitution.

description: Recursive formulas that express ∫sin^n(x)dx or ∫sec^n(x)dx in terms of integrals with lower powers.
hint: Use integration by parts to derive these formulas, then apply them repeatedly to reduce the exponent to a manageable base case.

description: Integrals like ∫sin(mx)cos(nx)dx solved using product-to-sum formulas.
hint: Convert the product to a sum using identities like sin(A)cos(B) = [sin(A+B)+sin(A-B)]/2, then integrate term by term.

description: Choosing when to use trigonometric methods versus other integration techniques for trigonometric integrands.
hint: Look at the form and powers first: odd powers suggest u-substitution, even powers suggest power-reducing, mixed products suggest product-to-sum.

description: A technique using trigonometric identities to eliminate square roots of the form √(a²-x²), √(a²+x²), or √(x²-a²) from integrands.
hint: Match the radical form to determine whether to use sine, tangent, or secant substitution based on the sign pattern.

description: The substitution x = a sin(θ) transforms √(a²-x²) into a cos(θ), eliminating the radical.
hint: Draw a right triangle with opposite side x and hypotenuse a to visualize why √(a²-x²) equals the adjacent side a cos(θ).

description: The substitution x = a tan(θ) transforms √(a²+x²) into a sec(θ) using the identity 1 + tan²(θ) = sec²(θ).
hint: Remember that dx = a sec²(θ) dθ, and use the tangent-secant identity to simplify the radical.

description: The substitution x = a sec(θ) transforms √(x²-a²) into a tan(θ) using the identity sec²(θ) - 1 = tan²(θ).
hint: Ensure x ≥ a or x ≤ -a for the square root to be defined, and restrict θ accordingly.

description: A right triangle constructed from the trigonometric substitution to convert the final answer back to the original variable x.
hint: Label the triangle sides based on your substitution equation, then read off the relationships you need for converting back.

description: Rewriting quadratic expressions ax² + bx + c in the form a(x-h)² + k to match standard substitution patterns.
hint: Factor out the coefficient of x² first, then complete the square on the remaining expression before identifying the substitution.

description: Using Pythagorean identities to simplify expressions after trigonometric substitution before integrating.
hint: After substitution, look for opportunities to apply sin²θ + cos²θ = 1, 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ, or sec²θ - 1 = tan²θ.

description: Appropriate restriction of θ to ensure the inverse trigonometric function is well-defined when back-substituting.
hint: For sine use [-π/2, π/2], for tangent use (-π/2, π/2), for secant use [0, π/2) ∪ (π/2, π] depending on sign of x.

description: A rational function P(x)/Q(x) can be expressed as a sum of simpler fractions when degree of P is less than degree of Q.
hint: First check if the numerator's degree is less than the denominator's; if not, perform polynomial long division first.

description: A rational function is proper if degree of numerator is less than degree of denominator, otherwise improper.
hint: Compare the highest power terms in numerator and denominator to determine if long division is needed.

description: For each linear factor (ax+b) in the denominator, include a term A/(ax+b) in the partial fraction decomposition.
hint: Each distinct linear factor gets its own constant numerator in the decomposition.

description: A repeated linear factor (ax+b)^n requires n terms: A₁/(ax+b) + A₂/(ax+b)² + ... + Aₙ/(ax+b)^n.
hint: For a factor raised to power n, you need n separate fractions with that factor raised to powers 1 through n.

description: For each irreducible quadratic factor ax²+bx+c, include a term (Ax+B)/(ax²+bx+c) with linear numerator.
hint: Irreducible quadratics need a full linear expression Ax+B in the numerator, not just a constant.

description: Multiply both sides by the common denominator to create an equation of polynomials for solving unknown coefficients.
hint: After multiplying through by the LCD, expand everything and collect like terms to match coefficients.

description: Choose strategic x-values that make specific terms zero to quickly solve for unknown coefficients.
hint: Substitute x-values that make factors equal zero (like roots of the denominator) to isolate individual constants.

description: Equate coefficients of like powers of x on both sides of the polynomial equation to create a system of equations.
hint: Match the coefficient of x², then x¹, then x⁰ on both sides to build equations for your unknowns.

description: Once decomposed, integrate each partial fraction term separately using logarithmic and algebraic integration rules.
hint: Linear denominators integrate to logarithms; use u-substitution when the denominator has a coefficient other than 1.

description: The process of analyzing an integral to determine which technique (substitution, parts, trigonometric, partial fractions) is most appropriate.
hint: Look for patterns: products suggest parts, compositions suggest substitution, radicals with squares suggest trigonometric substitution, and rational functions suggest partial fractions.

description: Algebraically manipulating an integrand using identities, factoring, or expansion to make it easier to integrate.
hint: Try expanding products, factoring expressions, or applying algebraic identities before choosing an integration technique.

description: Using multiple integration methods sequentially on the same problem, such as substitution followed by integration by parts.
hint: After applying one technique, reassess the new integral—it may require a different method to complete.

description: Using pre-computed integral formulas from tables to evaluate standard or complex integrals directly.
hint: Match your integral's form to table entries by first simplifying or making substitutions to fit standard patterns.

description: Using software tools like Mathematica or online calculators to evaluate integrals symbolically or numerically.
hint: Use CAS to verify your analytical work or to evaluate integrals that are too complex for standard techniques.

description: Identifying integrals that cannot be expressed in terms of elementary functions, such as ∫e^(-x²)dx or ∫(sin x)/x dx.
hint: If multiple techniques fail and the integral seems resistant to all methods, it may be non-elementary and require numerical or series methods.

description: A systematic approach to attempting integration methods in order: simplification, substitution, standard forms, advanced techniques, then tables or CAS.
hint: Start simple: check for direct substitution first, then progress to more complex techniques only if needed.

description: Approximating definite integrals using trapezoids formed by connecting consecutive function values with line segments.
hint: Remember the formula uses (b-a)/2n times the sum of first plus last values plus twice all middle values.

description: Approximating definite integrals using parabolic arcs through groups of three consecutive points, requiring an even number of subintervals.
hint: Apply the 1-4-2-4-2-4...-2-4-1 coefficient pattern and remember n must be even.

description: Maximum possible error formulas for approximation methods involving derivatives of the integrand and interval width.
hint: Find the maximum of the appropriate derivative on the interval to bound the error term.

description: How errors decrease as the number of subintervals increases, with different rates for different methods.
hint: Simpson's Rule converges faster than Trapezoidal Rule, which converges faster than basic Riemann sums.

description: Determining how many subintervals are needed to achieve a desired accuracy using error bound formulas.
hint: Set the error bound less than or equal to your desired accuracy and solve for n.

description: Applying numerical methods when antiderivatives cannot be expressed in elementary functions.
hint: If you can't find an antiderivative analytically, numerical methods may be your only practical option.

description: An integral with infinite limits of integration or an integrand with infinite discontinuities in the integration interval.
hint: Identify whether the problem is infinite limits or a discontinuous integrand before proceeding.

description: Improper integrals with one or both limits of integration being infinite (∫[a,∞), ∫(-∞,b], or ∫(-∞,∞)).
hint: Replace the infinite limit with a variable like t, evaluate the definite integral, then take the limit as t approaches infinity.

description: Improper integrals where the integrand has a vertical asymptote or discontinuity within the integration interval.
hint: Split the integral at the point of discontinuity and approach it with a limit from each side.

description: An improper integral converges if its limit exists and is finite; otherwise it diverges.
hint: After setting up the limit, evaluate it carefully—if you get a finite number, it converges; if infinite or undefined, it diverges.

description: If 0 ≤ f(x) ≤ g(x) and ∫g converges, then ∫f converges; if ∫f diverges, then ∫g diverges.
hint: Find a simpler function that bounds your integrand and whose convergence you already know.

description: If lim[x→∞] f(x)/g(x) = L where 0 < L < ∞, then ∫f and ∫g either both converge or both diverge.
hint: Choose a comparison function g(x) with similar behavior to f(x) as x approaches infinity, then compute the limit of their ratio.

description: The integral ∫[1,∞] 1/x^p dx converges if p > 1 and diverges if p ≤ 1.
hint: Rewrite your integrand in the form 1/x^p to quickly determine convergence by comparing p to 1.

description: Computing lim[t→∞] F(t) - F(a) or similar limits that arise when converting improper integrals to limit form.
hint: Apply L'Hôpital's rule if you encounter indeterminate forms, or use dominant term analysis for rational functions.

description: An equation relating a function to one or more of its derivatives.
hint: Look for equations containing y, y', y'', etc., not just algebraic equations in y alone.

description: The order of the highest derivative appearing in the differential equation.
hint: Find the highest derivative (y', y'', y''', etc.) and count the number of primes or derivative order.

description: A family of solutions containing arbitrary constants, representing all possible solutions.
hint: After solving, your answer should contain constants like C, C₁, C₂ that can take any value.

description: A specific solution obtained by using given initial values to determine arbitrary constants.
hint: Substitute the given point (x₀, y₀) into your general solution to solve for the constant C.

description: Confirming a proposed solution by substituting it and its derivatives into the original equation.
hint: Differentiate your proposed solution, substitute both y and y' into the equation, and check if both sides equal.

description: A differential equation paired with specified values of the function and possibly its derivatives at a given point.
hint: Look for conditions like y(0)=3 or y'(1)=2 given alongside the differential equation.

description: A graphical representation showing slope segments at grid points to visualize solution behavior.
hint: At each point (x,y), draw a short line segment with slope equal to f(x,y) from the equation y'=f(x,y).

description: First-order equations that can be written as g(y)dy = f(x)dx allowing separation of variables.
hint: Try to algebraically manipulate the equation so all y terms are with dy and all x terms are with dx.

description: A graphical representation where small line segments show the slope dy/dx at grid points in the xy-plane for a differential equation.
hint: At each point (x,y), draw a short segment with slope equal to f(x,y) from your differential equation dy/dx = f(x,y).

id: Solution Curve Sketching from Direction Field
description: Drawing curves through a direction field that are tangent to the slope segments at every point they pass through.
hint: Start at your initial condition and follow the direction of the slope segments, ensuring your curve is always tangent to them.

description: A numerical approximation technique that uses tangent line segments to step from one point to the next, approximating solutions to dy/dx = f(x,y).
hint: Use the formula y_{n+1} = y_n + h·f(x_n, y_n) where h is your step size and repeatedly apply it starting from your initial condition.

id: Step Size Selection in Euler's Method
description: Choosing the increment h for the independent variable that balances computational effort with accuracy in Euler's method.
hint: Smaller step sizes give better accuracy but require more calculations; try halving h to see if your answer stabilizes.

description: The error introduced in a single step of a numerical method, typically proportional to h² for Euler's method.
hint: The error at each step grows with the square of your step size, so halving h reduces single-step error by a factor of four.

description: The total accumulated error after many steps in a numerical method, typically proportional to h for Euler's method.
hint: Even though local error is O(h²), errors accumulate over approximately 1/h steps, giving total error proportional to h.

description: Curves along which the direction field has constant slope, formed by setting f(x,y) equal to a constant in dy/dx = f(x,y).
hint: To find where slopes equal a value like 2, solve f(x,y) = 2; all solution curves cross this curve with the same slope.

description: Constant solutions y = c where dy/dx = 0 for all x, appearing as horizontal lines in the direction field.
hint: Set f(x,y) = 0 and solve for y; these values give horizontal solution curves that other solutions may approach or avoid.

description: Rearranging a differential equation so all terms with one variable are on one side and all terms with the other variable are on the other side.
hint: Move all y terms (including dy) to one side and all x terms (including dx) to the other side before integrating.

description: Determining whether a differential equation can be written in the form g(y)dy = f(x)dx.
hint: Check if you can factor the equation into a product of a function of y only times a function of x only.

description: Integrating the separated equation with respect to each variable independently to find the general solution.
hint: After separating, place integral signs on both sides and integrate each side with respect to its own variable.

description: A solution to a differential equation where the dependent variable is not isolated but relates to the independent variable through an equation.
hint: If you cannot solve for y explicitly after integrating, leave your answer as an equation relating x and y.

description: The arbitrary constant arising from indefinite integration that represents the family of all solutions to a differential equation.
hint: Add the constant C after integrating both sides, or combine two constants into one on either side.

description: Isolating the dependent variable to express it as a function of the independent variable after integration.
hint: After integrating, use algebra to solve for y in terms of x when possible.

description: Solutions that may be eliminated when dividing both sides of an equation by an expression containing the dependent variable.
hint: Before dividing by a function of y, check if setting that function to zero gives an equilibrium solution.

id: Applying Initial Conditions to Separable DEs
description: Using given initial values to determine the specific constant and find a particular solution to a separable equation.
hint: Substitute the initial condition values into your general solution to solve for the constant C.

description: A first-order differential equation of the form dP/dt = rP(1 - P/K) modeling growth with carrying capacity.
hint: Identify the growth rate r and carrying capacity K from the equation structure before attempting to solve.

description: The maximum population size that an environment can sustain indefinitely in the logistic model.
hint: Look for the value that makes the growth rate zero when P equals that value.

description: The parameter representing the maximum per capita growth rate when population is small in the logistic model.
hint: This is the coefficient that determines how quickly the population approaches carrying capacity.

description: The technique of decomposing 1/(P(1-P/K)) into partial fractions to separate and integrate the logistic equation.
hint: Write 1/(P(K-P)) as A/P + B/(K-P) and solve for coefficients A and B.

description: The explicit solution P(t) = K/(1 + Ae^(-rt)) derived from solving the logistic differential equation.
hint: Use the initial condition P(0) = P₀ to find A = (K - P₀)/P₀, then substitute back.

description: The point where population growth rate changes from increasing to decreasing, occurring at P = K/2.
hint: Find where d²P/dt² = 0 by differentiating the logistic equation; maximum growth occurs at half the carrying capacity.

description: Understanding the three phases: slow initial growth, rapid exponential-like growth near K/2, and slow approach to K.
hint: Sketch the S-shaped curve and identify where dP/dt is largest by examining the factor P(1-P/K).

description: The constant solutions P = 0 and P = K where dP/dt = 0, representing stable and unstable equilibria.
hint: Set dP/dt = 0 and solve; P = K is stable (solutions approach it) while P = 0 is unstable.

id: Standard Form of First-Order Linear DE
description: A first-order differential equation written as dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x), where P and Q are functions of x only.
hint: Rearrange your equation so all y-terms are on the left with dy/dx, and move everything else to the right side.

description: A technique using μ(x) = e^(∫P(x)dx) to convert a linear DE into an exact form that can be integrated directly.
hint: First find P(x) from standard form, then compute the integrating factor by integrating P and exponentiating the result.

description: The process of calculating μ(x) = e^(∫P(x)dx) where the constant of integration can be omitted.
hint: You can ignore the constant C when finding ∫P(x)dx because it cancels out in the exponential.

id: Product Rule Recognition in Linear DEs
description: Identifying that μ(x)(dy/dx) + μ(x)P(x)y equals d/dx[μ(x)y] after multiplying by the integrating factor.
hint: After multiplying by μ(x), the left side should look like the derivative of a product—that's the key insight.

description: The process of integrating both sides of d/dx[μ(x)y] = μ(x)Q(x) and solving for y.
hint: After recognizing the product derivative form, integrate both sides with respect to x and don't forget the constant of integration.

description: Determining if a DE is linear by checking if y and its derivatives appear to the first power only, with no products or nonlinear functions of y.
hint: Check if y appears in products like y·y', inside functions like sin(y), or with powers like y²—any of these make it nonlinear.

id: Initial Condition Application for Linear DEs
description: Using a given point (x₀, y₀) to determine the constant of integration after solving a first-order linear equation.
hint: Substitute your initial values into the general solution and solve for C algebraically.

description: Converting a Bernoulli equation dy/dx + P(x)y = Q(x)y^n to linear form using the substitution v = y^(1-n).
hint: First identify n from the y^n term, then substitute v = y^(1-n) and use the chain rule to transform dy/dx.

description: A sequence is an ordered list of numbers a₁, a₂, a₃, ... defined by a function from positive integers to real numbers.
hint: Think of a sequence as a function where the input is n (1, 2, 3, ...) and the output is aₙ.

description: A formula that directly expresses the nth term aₙ in terms of n, like aₙ = 1/n or aₙ = 2ⁿ.
hint: To find a₅, simply substitute n=5 into your formula aₙ.

description: A formula that defines each term using previous terms, like aₙ₊₁ = 2aₙ with a₁ = 3.
hint: Start with the initial term and apply the recursion rule repeatedly to build later terms.

description: A sequence {aₙ} converges to limit L if for every ε > 0, there exists N such that |aₙ - L| < ε for all n ≥ N.
hint: Ask yourself: what value does aₙ approach as n gets arbitrarily large?

description: A sequence converges if its limit exists and is finite; otherwise it diverges (including oscillation or approaching infinity).
hint: Calculate lim(n→∞) aₙ; if you get a finite number, the sequence converges.

description: A sequence is bounded if there exist numbers M and m such that m ≤ aₙ ≤ M for all n.
hint: Check if all terms stay within some fixed interval [m, M]; graph several terms to visualize.

description: A sequence is monotonic if it is either always increasing (aₙ₊₁ ≥ aₙ) or always decreasing (aₙ₊₁ ≤ aₙ).
hint: Compare consecutive terms or check if aₙ₊₁ - aₙ has constant sign.

description: A bounded monotonic sequence always converges to a finite limit.
hint: If your sequence is both bounded and monotonic, you can guarantee convergence without finding the exact limit.

description: Limits of sequences follow algebraic rules: lim(aₙ + bₙ) = lim(aₙ) + lim(bₙ), and similar for products and quotients.
hint: Break complex sequences into simpler parts whose limits you know, then combine using limit laws.

description: An infinite series is the sum of the terms of an infinite sequence, written as Σ(n=1 to ∞) aₙ.
hint: Think of a series as adding up all terms of a sequence one after another without stopping.

description: The nth partial sum Sₙ is the sum of the first n terms of a series: Sₙ = Σ(k=1 to n) aₖ.
hint: Calculate S₁, S₂, S₃ by hand to see the pattern before looking at the limit.

description: A series Σaₙ converges if the sequence of partial sums {Sₙ} converges to a finite limit L.
hint: Check if your partial sums approach a specific number as n increases without bound.

description: A geometric series has the form Σ(n=0 to ∞) arⁿ and converges to a/(1-r) when |r| < 1.
hint: Identify the first term a and common ratio r, then check if |r| < 1 before using the formula.

description: If lim(n→∞) aₙ ≠ 0 or does not exist, then the series Σaₙ diverges.
hint: Always check this test first—it's the easiest way to prove divergence, but can't prove convergence.

description: The harmonic series Σ(n=1 to ∞) 1/n diverges despite its terms approaching zero.
hint: This classic counterexample shows that lim aₙ = 0 does not guarantee convergence.

description: A telescoping series has partial sums where intermediate terms cancel, leaving only first and last terms.
hint: Write out several partial sums explicitly to identify which terms cancel in the middle.

description: If Σaₙ and Σbₙ converge, then Σ(aₙ ± bₙ) converges and Σ(caₙ) converges for any constant c.
hint: You can add convergent series term-by-term and factor out constants, just like finite sums.

description: If the limit of the nth term is not zero, then the series diverges.
hint: Check if lim(n→∞) aₙ = 0; if not, the series must diverge immediately.

description: A series converges if and only if the corresponding improper integral converges, when the function is positive, continuous, and decreasing.
hint: Replace the sum with an integral from 1 to infinity and check if that improper integral converges.

description: The function f(x) must be positive, continuous, and decreasing on [1,∞) for the integral test to apply.
hint: Verify all three conditions before applying the integral test: check positivity, continuity, and take the derivative to confirm decreasing.

description: The series Σ(1/n^p) converges if p > 1 and diverges if p ≤ 1.
hint: Identify the exponent p in the denominator; convergence depends solely on whether p exceeds 1.

description: The remainder of a series can be bounded using integrals when the integral test conditions hold.
hint: Use the integral of f from n+1 to infinity to bound the tail of the series after n terms.

description: Evaluating the improper integral ∫₁^∞ f(x)dx determines whether the corresponding series converges.
hint: Set up the limit as b→∞ of the definite integral from 1 to b, then evaluate the antiderivative.

description: A series can be visualized as rectangles approximating the area under the curve of its corresponding function.
hint: Sketch the decreasing function and rectangles to see why the series and integral have the same convergence behavior.

description: If 0 ≤ aₙ ≤ bₙ for all n, then Σbₙ converges implies Σaₙ converges, and Σaₙ diverges implies Σbₙ diverges.
hint: Find a larger series that converges or a smaller series that diverges, then verify the inequality holds for all terms.

description: Selecting an appropriate known series (geometric, p-series, or harmonic) to compare with the given series.
hint: Identify the dominant term in aₙ as n→∞, then construct a geometric or p-series that matches that behavior.

description: Proving algebraically that 0 ≤ aₙ ≤ bₙ holds for all n beyond some N.
hint: Factor, simplify, or estimate each term carefully, and remember you only need the inequality to hold eventually, not necessarily for small n.

description: If lim(n→∞) aₙ/bₙ = L where 0 < L < ∞, then Σaₙ and Σbₙ either both converge or both diverge.
hint: When terms are too complicated to compare directly, compute the limit of their ratio instead of establishing inequalities.

description: Evaluating limits of ratios aₙ/bₙ using algebraic manipulation or L'Hôpital's rule for corresponding functions.
hint: Divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of n, or convert to continuous functions and apply L'Hôpital's rule.

description: Identifying the fastest-growing terms in numerator and denominator to simplify series comparison.
hint: As n→∞, polynomial terms dominate constants, exponentials dominate polynomials, and factorials dominate exponentials.

description: Recognizing when comparison tests fail: when L=0 or L=∞ in LCT, or when inequality goes the wrong direction in DCT.
hint: If your comparison gives L=0 or L=∞, try a different comparison series; you need 0 < L < ∞ for the Limit Comparison Test.

description: For series with rational functions, comparing to p-series based on the degree difference between numerator and denominator.
hint: If numerator has degree m and denominator has degree n, compare to 1/n^(n-m); it converges when n-m > 1.

description: An infinite series whose terms alternate in sign, typically written as ∑(-1)^n·b_n or ∑(-1)^(n+1)·b_n where b_n > 0.
hint: Look for the (-1)^n factor that flips the sign of each successive term.

description: A series ∑(-1)^n·b_n converges if b_n is decreasing, positive, and lim(n→∞) b_n = 0.
hint: Check all three conditions: positive terms, decreasing sequence, and limit zero—all must hold.

description: Showing b_(n+1) ≤ b_n for all n, often using derivatives or algebraic manipulation.
hint: Try comparing b_(n+1) to b_n directly, or use f'(x) < 0 where f(n) = b_n.

description: The error |S - S_N| when approximating alternating series sum S by partial sum S_N is bounded by |b_(N+1)|.
hint: The first omitted term gives you the maximum error—no need to compute all remaining terms.

description: A series ∑a_n converges absolutely if ∑|a_n| converges.
hint: Remove all negative signs and test the resulting positive series with comparison, ratio, or other tests.

description: A series ∑a_n converges conditionally if it converges but ∑|a_n| diverges.
hint: First verify the original series converges (often by Alternating Series Test), then show the absolute version diverges.

description: Understanding that absolute convergence implies convergence, but conditional convergence is weaker.
hint: If ∑|a_n| converges, you're done—the original series converges; otherwise check if it's conditionally convergent.

description: The series ∑(-1)^(n+1)/n = 1 - 1/2 + 1/3 - 1/4 + ... which converges conditionally to ln(2).
hint: This is the classic example: converges by Alternating Series Test but diverges absolutely since it's the harmonic series.

description: A convergence test that examines the limit of the ratio of consecutive terms |a_{n+1}/a_n| to determine if a series converges absolutely, diverges, or is inconclusive.
hint: Calculate L = lim(n→∞)|a_{n+1}/a_n|: if L<1 converges absolutely, L>1 diverges, L=1 inconclusive.

description: A convergence test that examines the limit of the nth root of absolute terms lim(n→∞)|a_n|^(1/n) to determine absolute convergence or divergence.
hint: Calculate L = lim(n→∞)|a_n|^(1/n): if L<1 converges absolutely, L>1 diverges, L=1 inconclusive.

description: Series containing factorial expressions n! in numerator or denominator, which grow extremely rapidly and often make the Ratio Test effective.
hint: When you see factorials, use Ratio Test because (n+1)!/n! simplifies to (n+1).

description: Series containing exponential expressions like r^n where the base changes with n, making the Ratio or Root Test particularly effective.
hint: For series with r^n where r varies with n, try Root Test to extract the base directly.

description: When the Ratio Test yields L=1, the test provides no information and another convergence test must be applied.
hint: If Ratio Test gives L=1, try Comparison Test, Integral Test, or p-Series Test instead.

description: The algebraic technique of simplifying |a_{n+1}/a_n| by canceling common factors and reducing expressions before taking limits.
hint: Before taking the limit, cancel factorials using (n+1)!/n! = n+1 and simplify exponentials using laws of exponents.

description: The Root Test is particularly efficient for series where terms have the form (f(n))^n because the nth root directly extracts f(n).
hint: If your term looks like (expression)^n, Root Test will reduce |a_n|^(1/n) to just |expression|.

id: Choosing Between Ratio and Root Tests
description: The strategic decision of which test to apply based on series structure: Ratio Test for factorials and products, Root Test for exponential forms.
hint: Use Ratio Test for factorials or products of terms; use Root Test when the entire term is raised to the nth power.

description: An infinite series of the form Σ(n=0 to ∞) cₙ(x-a)ⁿ where cₙ are coefficients and a is the center.
hint: Start by identifying the coefficients cₙ and the center point a in your series.

description: The value R such that a power series converges for |x-a| < R and diverges for |x-a| > R.
hint: Apply the ratio test or root test to |cₙ(x-a)ⁿ| to find where the limit is less than 1.

description: The set of all x-values for which a power series converges, including endpoint behavior.
hint: After finding the radius R, separately test convergence at both endpoints x = a-R and x = a+R.

description: A power series defines a function f(x) = Σcₙ(x-a)ⁿ on its interval of convergence.
hint: Evaluate the function by substituting specific x-values within the interval of convergence.

description: A power series can be differentiated term-by-term: d/dx[Σcₙ(x-a)ⁿ] = Σn·cₙ(x-a)ⁿ⁻¹ within its radius of convergence.
hint: Differentiate each term separately using the power rule, noting the radius of convergence stays the same.

description: A power series can be integrated term-by-term: ∫Σcₙ(x-a)ⁿdx = C + Σ[cₙ/(n+1)](x-a)ⁿ⁺¹ within its radius of convergence.
hint: Integrate each term using the power rule for integrals, remembering to add a constant of integration.

description: The process of separately testing convergence at x = a-R and x = a+R using appropriate series tests.
hint: Substitute each endpoint value into the series and apply tests like alternating series test or p-series test.

description: A power series of the form Σcₙxⁿ centered at the origin (Maclaurin-type form).
hint: When the center a=0, simplify by writing the series as Σcₙxⁿ and test |x| < R.

description: Power series can be added, subtracted, and multiplied term-by-term within their common interval of convergence.
hint: Find where both series converge, then combine coefficients of like powers of x.

description: Algebraic operations on power series preserve convergence within the smaller radius of convergence.
hint: The resulting series converges at least where both original series converge simultaneously.

description: Replacing the variable in a power series with another expression to generate new series representations.
hint: Substitute the expression for x everywhere, then simplify and check the new interval of convergence.

description: Term-by-term differentiation of a power series maintains the same radius of convergence but may affect endpoint behavior.
hint: The radius stays the same, but always recheck convergence at the endpoints after differentiating.

description: Term-by-term integration of a power series maintains the same radius of convergence but may affect endpoint behavior.
hint: The radius stays the same, but always recheck convergence at the endpoints after integrating.

description: Using known power series and algebraic operations to represent new functions as power series.
hint: Start with geometric series or known expansions, then apply substitution, differentiation, or integration.

description: If a function equals a power series on an interval, the coefficients are uniquely determined by the function's derivatives.
hint: Two different power series cannot represent the same function on an interval around their center.

description: Using addition, multiplication, and composition to build complex function representations from simpler power series.
hint: Break the target function into operations on functions with known series, then combine appropriately.

description: An infinite series representation of a function as a sum of terms calculated from the function's derivatives at a single point.
hint: Start with the general formula: f(x) = Σ(f^(n)(a)/n!)(x-a)^n and identify what each part means for your specific function.

description: A Taylor series centered at a=0, given by f(x) = Σ(f^(n)(0)/n!)(x^n).
hint: This is just a Taylor series with a=0; compute derivatives at zero to find coefficients.

description: Finding the nth coefficient of a Taylor series by evaluating the nth derivative at the center point and dividing by n factorial.
hint: Calculate f'(a), f''(a), f'''(a), etc., then divide each by the corresponding factorial to get each coefficient.

description: Standard Maclaurin series for elementary functions including e^x, sin(x), cos(x), and 1/(1-x).
hint: Memorize the basic series for e^x, sin(x), cos(x), and geometric series; you'll use them to build more complex ones.

description: Using the first n+1 terms of a Taylor series as a polynomial approximation T_n(x) to the original function.
hint: Truncate the infinite series after the x^n term to get a manageable polynomial that approximates your function near the center.

description: A formula for the error R_n(x) between a function and its nth Taylor polynomial, often expressed using the (n+1)st derivative.
hint: The remainder tells you how far off your polynomial approximation is; bound it by finding the maximum of |f^(n+1)| on your interval.

description: Creating new Taylor series by performing algebraic operations, substitution, differentiation, or integration on known series.
hint: Start with a series you know (like e^x or geometric series), then substitute, differentiate, or integrate term-by-term to match your target function.

id: Interval of Convergence for Taylor Series
description: Determining the set of x-values for which a Taylor series converges to the function it represents.
hint: Use the ratio test or other convergence tests you already know, then check endpoints separately if needed.

description: Replacing the variable in a known Taylor series with another expression to find new series representations.
hint: Identify the known series pattern, substitute your expression for x, then simplify each term carefully.

description: Finding Taylor series by substituting expressions like x² or -x into standard series like eˣ or sin(x).
hint: Start with the standard series, replace every occurrence of x with your new expression, then adjust the radius of convergence.

description: Finding the product of two power series by multiplying terms and collecting like powers.
hint: Multiply the first few terms from each series, group terms with the same power of x, and look for a pattern.

description: Finding the quotient of two power series by long division or algebraic manipulation.
hint: Use long division of polynomials technique, or multiply numerator by the reciprocal series if you know it.

description: Finding Taylor series by differentiating a known series representation term by term.
hint: Differentiate each term of the known series, remember to adjust coefficients and powers according to the power rule.

description: Finding Taylor series by integrating a known series representation term by term and adding a constant.
hint: Integrate each term separately, increase each power by 1 and divide by the new power, then determine the constant from initial conditions.

description: Evaluating definite integrals by integrating a Taylor series term by term within its interval of convergence.
hint: Replace the integrand with its Taylor series, integrate term by term, then substitute the limits into the resulting series.

description: Finding power series solutions to differential equations by substituting a series and matching coefficients.
hint: Assume a solution is a power series, substitute it and its derivatives into the equation, then equate coefficients of like powers.

description: A pair of equations x=f(t) and y=g(t) that express coordinates as functions of a parameter t.
hint: Think of t as time and trace how a point moves as t changes from one value to another.

description: Solving for t in one parametric equation and substituting into the other to obtain a Cartesian equation y=F(x).
hint: Isolate t from the simpler equation first, then substitute that expression into the other equation.

description: The direction a curve is traced as the parameter t increases.
hint: Plot points for increasing values of t (like t=0, 1, 2) and draw arrows showing the path direction.

description: Standard form x=h+r·cos(t), y=k+r·sin(t) describes a circle with center (h,k) and radius r.
hint: Use the Pythagorean identity cos²(t)+sin²(t)=1 to verify the curve is a circle.

description: Linear parametric form x=x₀+at, y=y₀+bt describes a line through (x₀,y₀) with direction vector ⟨a,b⟩.
hint: Eliminate t to find slope m=b/a if a≠0, or recognize vertical line if a=0.

description: Different parametric equations can describe the same geometric curve with different speeds or orientations.
hint: After elimination, check if you get the same Cartesian equation and compare orientations for specific t values.

description: The interval for parameter t determines which portion of a curve is traced.
hint: Evaluate the parametric equations at the endpoints of the t-interval to find where the curve starts and stops.

description: Expressing a Cartesian equation as parametric equations by choosing x=t or y=t and solving for the other variable.
hint: Let x=t, then solve for y in terms of t, or choose a parametrization that simplifies the given equation.

description: Finding dy/dx for curves defined by x=f(t), y=g(t) using the formula dy/dx = (dy/dt)/(dx/dt).
hint: Differentiate both x and y with respect to t separately, then divide dy/dt by dx/dt.

description: Computing d²y/dx² for parametric curves using the formula d²y/dx² = (d/dt(dy/dx))/(dx/dt).
hint: First find dy/dx, then differentiate that result with respect to t and divide by dx/dt again.

description: Finding the length of a parametric curve from t=a to t=b using the integral √[(dx/dt)² + (dy/dt)²] dt.
hint: Square both derivatives, add them, take the square root, and integrate over the parameter interval.

description: Computing surface area when revolving a parametric curve around an axis using 2π times radius times arc length element.
hint: Identify which variable (x or y) is the radius, multiply by the parametric arc length formula, and integrate.

description: Finding equations of tangent lines at specific parameter values using the parametric derivative dy/dx.
hint: Evaluate both x(t) and y(t) at the given t to get a point, then find dy/dx at that t for the slope.

description: Identifying where parametric curves have horizontal tangents (dy/dt=0, dx/dt≠0) or vertical tangents (dx/dt=0, dy/dt≠0).
hint: Horizontal tangents occur when the numerator dy/dt equals zero; vertical when the denominator dx/dt equals zero.

description: Determining concavity using the sign of d²y/dx² for parametric equations.
hint: If d²y/dx² is positive, the curve is concave up; if negative, concave down at that parameter value.

description: A coordinate system where each point is determined by a distance r from the origin and an angle θ from the positive x-axis.
hint: Draw a point, measure its distance from origin (r) and angle counterclockwise from the positive x-axis (θ).

description: Using the formulas x = r cos(θ) and y = r sin(θ) to convert polar coordinates to rectangular coordinates.
hint: Remember that x equals r times cosine of theta, and y equals r times sine of theta.

description: Using r² = x² + y² and tan(θ) = y/x to convert rectangular coordinates to polar form.
hint: Find r using the Pythagorean theorem, then find θ using arctangent while checking the quadrant.

description: Equations of the form r = f(θ) that describe curves in the polar coordinate system.
hint: Plug in different θ values to find corresponding r values and plot points to see the curve shape.

description: Testing polar equations for symmetry about the x-axis, y-axis, or origin by substituting -θ, π-θ, or -r.
hint: Replace θ with -θ to test x-axis symmetry; if the equation stays the same, the graph is symmetric.

description: Standard polar curves including circles (r = a), cardioids (r = a(1 ± cos θ)), and rose curves (r = a cos(nθ)).
hint: For cardioids, the plus or minus determines direction; for roses, n petals if n is odd, 2n if n is even.

description: Computing dy/dx for polar curves using dy/dx = (dr/dθ sin θ + r cos θ)/(dr/dθ cos θ - r sin θ).
hint: Use the chain rule with x = r cos θ and y = r sin θ to find dy/dθ and dx/dθ first.

description: Finding slopes and equations of tangent lines to curves given in polar form at specific points.
hint: Calculate dy/dx at the given θ value using the polar derivative formula, then use point-slope form.

description: The area of a region bounded by a polar curve r=f(θ) from θ=α to θ=β is A = (1/2)∫[α to β] r² dθ.
hint: Square the radius function r(θ) first, then integrate; the 1/2 factor comes from the sector area formula.
section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

description: The area between outer curve r=f(θ) and inner curve r=g(θ) is A = (1/2)∫[α to β] (f(θ)² - g(θ)²) dθ.
hint: Subtract the inner radius squared from the outer radius squared before integrating, keeping the 1/2 factor outside.
section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

description: Determining the bounds α and β by analyzing where the curve begins and ends or where curves intersect.
hint: Sketch the polar curve and identify the starting and ending angles; for intersections, solve f(θ) = g(θ).
section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

description: The arc length of polar curve r=f(θ) from θ=α to θ=β is L = ∫[α to β] √(r² + (dr/dθ)²) dθ.
hint: Compute dr/dθ first, then form the expression under the square root; this formula comes from the parametric arc length formula.
section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

description: Finding the derivative of the radius function r(θ) with respect to the parameter θ.
hint: Treat θ as the variable and differentiate r(θ) using standard differentiation rules.
section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

id: Area of One Petal or Loop
description: Computing the area of a single petal in rose curves or one loop in limaçons by identifying appropriate integration bounds.
hint: One petal typically spans from where r=0 to the next zero; for r=sin(nθ), one petal covers π/n radians.
section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

description: Using symmetry properties to compute total area by integrating over one symmetric portion and multiplying.
hint: If the curve has symmetry about the polar axis or θ=π/2, integrate over one section and multiply by the number of symmetric parts.
section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

description: Finding where two polar curves meet by solving r₁(θ) = r₂(θ) and checking for the pole (origin).
hint: Remember the origin can be represented by r=0 at any angle; check both algebraic intersections and when each curve passes through the pole.
section: 1.4 Area and Arc Length in Polar Coordinates

description: A parabola has equation (x-h)²=4p(y-k) or (y-k)²=4p(x-h) with vertex (h,k) and focal parameter p.
hint: Identify which variable is squared to determine if the parabola opens vertically or horizontally.

description: An ellipse has equation (x-h)²/a² + (y-k)²/b² = 1 with center (h,k), semi-major axis a, and semi-minor axis b.
hint: The larger denominator determines the major axis direction; use c²=a²-b² to find foci.

description: A hyperbola has equation (x-h)²/a² - (y-k)²/b² = 1 or (y-k)²/a² - (x-h)²/b² = 1 with center (h,k).
hint: The positive term determines the transverse axis direction; use c²=a²+b² for hyperbolas.

description: Eccentricity e measures conic shape: e=0 for circles, 0<e<1 for ellipses, e=1 for parabolas, e>1 for hyperbolas.
hint: Calculate e=c/a where c is the focal distance and a is the semi-major axis length.

description: Conics with focus at origin have polar form r = ed/(1 ± e·cosθ) or r = ed/(1 ± e·sinθ) where e is eccentricity.
hint: The denominator form (cosθ or sinθ, plus or minus) determines directrix orientation and position.

description: A directrix is a fixed line such that the ratio of distances (point to focus)/(point to directrix) equals eccentricity e.
hint: For polar conics, the constant d in the equation represents the distance from focus to directrix.

description: Transform general conic equation Ax²+Cy²+Dx+Ey+F=0 to standard form by completing the square in x and y.
hint: Group x-terms and y-terms separately, complete the square for each, then isolate the constant on the right.

description: Determine conic type from Ax²+Bxy+Cy²+Dx+Ey+F=0 using discriminant B²-4AC or coefficient pattern.
hint: If B=0: same sign coefficients mean ellipse, opposite signs mean hyperbola, one squared term means parabola.

description: Transform standard Cartesian conic equations into polar form using x=r·cosθ and y=r·sinθ substitutions.
hint: After substituting, solve for r as a function of θ by factoring r from all terms.

description: A vector is a mathematical object with magnitude and direction, represented geometrically as an arrow from initial point to terminal point.
hint: Draw the arrow from the starting point to the ending point; the arrow's length and direction fully describe the vector.

description: A vector in the plane is written as ⟨a, b⟩ where a and b are the horizontal and vertical components.
hint: Subtract initial point coordinates from terminal point coordinates to find components: ⟨x₂-x₁, y₂-y₁⟩.

description: The magnitude (or length) of vector v = ⟨a, b⟩ is ||v|| = √(a² + b²).
hint: Use the Pythagorean theorem on the components to find the distance from the origin to the point (a, b).

description: Vectors are added or subtracted component-wise: ⟨a₁, b₁⟩ ± ⟨a₂, b₂⟩ = ⟨a₁ ± a₂, b₁ ± b₂⟩.
hint: Add or subtract corresponding components separately; geometrically, place vectors tip-to-tail for addition.

description: Multiplying a vector by a scalar k scales its magnitude: k⟨a, b⟩ = ⟨ka, kb⟩.
hint: Multiply each component by the scalar; negative scalars reverse direction while positive ones preserve it.

description: A unit vector has magnitude 1; any vector v can be normalized to u = v/||v||.
hint: Divide each component by the vector's magnitude to get a unit vector in the same direction.

id: Standard Unit Vectors i and j
description: The standard unit vectors are i = ⟨1, 0⟩ and j = ⟨0, 1⟩, allowing any vector to be written as v = ai + bj.
hint: Express vectors as combinations of i and j: the coefficients are just the x and y components.

description: The zero vector 0 = ⟨0, 0⟩ has magnitude zero and indeterminate direction, serving as the additive identity.
hint: The zero vector is the only vector with magnitude zero; adding it to any vector leaves that vector unchanged.

description: A system using three perpendicular axes (x, y, z) to locate points in space with ordered triples (x, y, z).
hint: Draw the axes with x and y in the horizontal plane and z pointing upward, forming right angles.

description: Directed line segments in space represented as ordered triples ⟨a, b, c⟩ or ai + bj + ck.
hint: Extend your 2D vector thinking by adding a third component for the z-direction.

description: The length of a 3D vector v = ⟨a, b, c⟩ calculated as ||v|| = √(a² + b² + c²).
hint: Use the Pythagorean theorem twice: first in the xy-plane, then include the z-component.

id: Standard Unit Vectors i j k
description: The vectors i = ⟨1, 0, 0⟩, j = ⟨0, 1, 0⟩, and k = ⟨0, 0, 1⟩ pointing along the coordinate axes.
hint: These are just the 3D extension of i and j, with k pointing along the z-axis.

description: Addition, subtraction, and scalar multiplication performed component-wise on three-dimensional vectors.
hint: Apply the same rules as 2D vectors but include the third component in all calculations.

description: The distance between points (x₁, y₁, z₁) and (x₂, y₂, z₂) is √[(x₂-x₁)² + (y₂-y₁)² + (z₂-z₁)²].
hint: This is the magnitude of the vector from one point to the other.

description: The midpoint between (x₁, y₁, z₁) and (x₂, y₂, z₂) is ((x₁+x₂)/2, (y₁+y₂)/2, (z₁+z₂)/2).
hint: Average each coordinate separately, just like in 2D but with a third coordinate.

description: A sphere with center (h, k, l) and radius r has equation (x-h)² + (y-k)² + (z-l)² = r².
hint: This is the 3D version of a circle equation using the distance formula from the center.

description: The dot product of vectors u and v is u·v = u₁v₁ + u₂v₂ + u₃v₃, yielding a scalar.
hint: Multiply corresponding components and add them together to get a single number, not a vector.

description: The dot product equals |u||v|cos(θ), where θ is the angle between vectors u and v.
hint: Use this form when you know magnitudes and angle instead of components.

description: The angle θ between vectors satisfies cos(θ) = (u·v)/(|u||v|), where 0 ≤ θ ≤ π.
hint: Calculate the dot product and magnitudes first, then use inverse cosine to find the angle.

description: Two nonzero vectors are orthogonal (perpendicular) if and only if their dot product equals zero.
hint: Check if u·v = 0; if yes, the vectors form a 90-degree angle.

description: The scalar projection of u onto v is comp_v(u) = (u·v)/|v|, and the vector projection is proj_v(u) = [(u·v)/|v|²]v.
hint: Find how much of vector u points in the direction of v by computing the dot product divided by v's magnitude.

description: The dot product is commutative (u·v = v·u), distributive over addition, and satisfies c(u·v) = (cu)·v for scalar c.
hint: Use these algebraic properties to simplify expressions before computing components.

description: Work W equals F·d, the dot product of force vector F and displacement vector d.
hint: Only the component of force in the direction of motion contributes to work.

description: For a vector v, the direction cosines are cos(α), cos(β), cos(γ), where α, β, γ are angles with the positive x, y, z axes.
hint: Calculate direction cosines using dot products with standard unit vectors i, j, k.

description: The cross product of two vectors in three dimensions produces a third vector perpendicular to both input vectors.
hint: Use the determinant formula with i, j, k unit vectors or memorize the component formula: (a₂b₃-a₃b₂)i - (a₁b₃-a₃b₁)j + (a₁b₂-a₂b₁)k.

description: A method to determine the direction of the cross product vector using the right hand.
hint: Point your fingers along the first vector, curl them toward the second vector, and your thumb points in the direction of the cross product.

description: The magnitude |u × v| equals |u||v|sin(θ) where θ is the angle between the vectors.
hint: This formula connects to area: the magnitude gives the area of the parallelogram formed by the two vectors.

description: The cross product is anticommutative (u × v = -(v × u)), distributive, and u × u = 0.
hint: Remember order matters: swapping vectors reverses the direction of the result.

description: Two vectors are parallel if and only if their cross product equals the zero vector.
hint: Check if u × v = 0; this happens when the vectors point in the same or opposite directions.

id: Area of Parallelogram Using Cross Product
description: The area of a parallelogram spanned by vectors u and v equals |u × v|.
hint: Compute the cross product first, then find its magnitude to get the area.

id: Area of Triangle Using Cross Product
description: The area of a triangle with sides formed by vectors u and v equals (1/2)|u × v|.
hint: A triangle is half a parallelogram, so take half the magnitude of the cross product.

description: Torque τ equals r × F, where r is the position vector and F is the force vector.
hint: The magnitude of torque is |r||F|sin(θ), representing the rotational effect of the force.

description: The scalar u · (v × w) gives the volume of the parallelepiped formed by three vectors.
hint: Compute the cross product v × w first, then take the dot product with u.

id: Vector Equation of a Line in Space
description: A line in 3D space represented as r(t) = r₀ + tv, where r₀ is a position vector to a point on the line and v is a direction vector.
hint: Start with a known point on the line and add a scalar multiple of the direction vector to reach any other point.
section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

description: Component form of a line in space written as x = x₀ + at, y = y₀ + bt, z = z₀ + ct, where (a,b,c) is the direction vector.
hint: Extract each component from the vector equation separately to get three scalar equations.
section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

description: Eliminating the parameter t from parametric equations to get (x-x₀)/a = (y-y₀)/b = (z-z₀)/c, valid when direction components are nonzero.
hint: Solve each parametric equation for t and set them equal to eliminate the parameter.
section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

description: A vector perpendicular to every vector lying in a given plane, used to determine the plane's orientation in space.
hint: Use the cross product of two vectors in the plane to find a normal vector.
section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

description: A plane represented as ax + by + cz = d, where (a,b,c) is a normal vector to the plane.
hint: Use the dot product of the normal vector with a general point (x,y,z) minus a known point on the plane, then expand.
section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

description: The perpendicular distance from a point to a plane, calculated using the formula |ax₁ + by₁ + cz₁ - d|/√(a² + b² + c²).
hint: Substitute the point coordinates into the plane equation, take absolute value, and divide by the magnitude of the normal vector.
section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

description: The angle between two planes equals the angle between their normal vectors, found using the dot product formula.
hint: Extract normal vectors from each plane equation and use the dot product angle formula.
section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

id: Parallel and Perpendicular Lines in Space
description: Two lines in space are parallel if their direction vectors are scalar multiples, and perpendicular if their direction vectors have zero dot product.
hint: Compare direction vectors using scalar multiplication for parallel or dot product for perpendicular.
section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

description: The shortest distance between two non-intersecting, non-parallel lines in space, calculated using the scalar triple product formula.
hint: Use the formula |((P₂ - P₁) · (v₁ × v₂))/|v₁ × v₂|| where P₁, P₂ are points on each line and v₁, v₂ are direction vectors.
section: 2.5 Equations of Lines and Planes in Space

description: A three-dimensional surface defined by a second-degree equation in x, y, and z.
hint: Look for squared terms in all three variables with no products like xy or xz.

description: A quadric surface with equation (x²/a²) + (y²/b²) + (z²/c²) = 1, bounded in all directions.
hint: All terms are positive and equal to 1; slice with planes parallel to coordinate planes to see ellipses.

description: A quadric surface with form (x²/a²) + (y²/b²) - (z²/c²) = 1, having one negative squared term.
hint: One minus sign creates a connected surface; horizontal slices are ellipses that grow with |z|.

description: A quadric surface with form -(x²/a²) - (y²/b²) + (z²/c²) = 1, having two negative squared terms and two separate parts.
hint: Two minus signs create two disconnected sheets; exists only where the positive term is large enough.

description: A quadric surface with form z = (x²/a²) + (y²/b²), shaped like an upward or downward bowl.
hint: One variable is linear while two are squared with the same sign; cross-sections parallel to xy-plane are ellipses.

description: A saddle-shaped quadric surface with form z = (x²/a²) - (y²/b²), having opposite-signed squared terms.
hint: Also called a saddle surface; trace curves in xz and yz planes have opposite concavity.

description: A quadric surface with form (x²/a²) + (y²/b²) = (z²/c²), forming a double cone with vertex at origin.
hint: Right side has only z² with no constant; horizontal slices are ellipses whose size grows linearly with |z|.

description: Curves formed by intersecting a quadric surface with coordinate planes or planes parallel to them.
hint: Set one variable to a constant and analyze the resulting 2D curve to understand the 3D shape.

description: The process of identifying quadric surface type by examining signs, degrees, and presence of constant terms.
hint: Count how many variables are squared, check if equation equals constant or variable, and note signs of terms.

description: A point in space is represented by (r, θ, z) where r is distance from z-axis, θ is angle from positive x-axis, and z is height.
hint: Draw the projection onto the xy-plane first, then add the z-coordinate as vertical height.

description: Transform cylindrical coordinates (r, θ, z) to rectangular using x = r cos θ, y = r sin θ, and z = z.
hint: Use the same trigonometric relationships as polar coordinates for x and y, then keep z unchanged.

description: Transform rectangular coordinates (x, y, z) to cylindrical using r = √(x² + y²), tan θ = y/x, and z = z.
hint: Calculate r using the distance formula in the xy-plane, find θ as you would in polar coordinates, and preserve z.

description: A point in space is represented by (ρ, θ, φ) where ρ is distance from origin, θ is azimuthal angle, and φ is angle from positive z-axis.
hint: Remember ρ is always nonnegative, φ ranges from 0 to π, and θ ranges from 0 to 2π.

description: Transform spherical coordinates (ρ, θ, φ) to rectangular using x = ρ sin φ cos θ, y = ρ sin φ sin θ, and z = ρ cos φ.
hint: Start with z = ρ cos φ, then use ρ sin φ as the projection radius in the xy-plane for x and y.

description: Transform rectangular coordinates (x, y, z) to spherical using ρ = √(x² + y² + z²), tan θ = y/x, and cos φ = z/ρ.
hint: Calculate ρ using the 3D distance formula, find φ from the z-component, then determine θ in the xy-plane.

description: Equations in cylindrical coordinates where one variable is constant or absent describe surfaces like circular cylinders (r = constant) or vertical planes (θ = constant).
hint: If r is constant, visualize a cylinder around the z-axis; if θ is constant, imagine a half-plane extending from the z-axis.

description: Equations in spherical coordinates where one variable is constant describe surfaces like spheres (ρ = constant), cones (φ = constant), or half-planes (θ = constant).
hint: If ρ is constant, you get a sphere; if φ is constant, you get a cone opening from the origin.

description: Transform between cylindrical (r, θ, z) and spherical (ρ, θ, φ) using r = ρ sin φ and z = ρ cos φ, with θ the same in both.
hint: Notice θ is identical in both systems; relate r and z to ρ and φ using right triangle relationships.

description: A function that maps a real parameter t to a vector in space, typically written as r(t) = ⟨f(t), g(t), h(t)⟩ or r(t) = f(t)i + g(t)j + h(t)k.
hint: Start by identifying the component functions f(t), g(t), and h(t) that give the x, y, and z coordinates respectively.

description: The set of all terminal points of position vectors r(t) as the parameter t varies, forming a curve traced through three-dimensional space.
hint: Think of t as time: as t increases, the tip of the vector r(t) traces out a path in space.

description: The set of all parameter values t for which all component functions f(t), g(t), h(t) are defined, typically the intersection of their individual domains.
hint: Find the domain of each component separately, then take the intersection of all three domains.

description: The limit of r(t) as t approaches a exists if the limits of all component functions exist, calculated component-wise: lim r(t) = ⟨lim f(t), lim g(t), lim h(t)⟩.
hint: Evaluate the limit separately for each component function using standard limit techniques.

description: A vector-valued function r(t) is continuous at t = a if lim(t→a) r(t) = r(a), which occurs when all component functions are continuous at a.
hint: Check continuity of each component function individually; r(t) is continuous only if all components are continuous.

description: A space curve of form r(t) = ⟨a cos(t), a sin(t), bt⟩ that spirals around a cylinder with constant radius a and vertical rate b.
hint: The xy-components trace a circle while the z-component increases linearly, creating a spiral staircase shape.

description: The process of expressing a curve in space using a parameter t, where different parameterizations can describe the same geometric curve.
hint: Multiple parameter choices can trace the same curve; consider how changing t affects the speed and direction of traversal.

description: The limit of the difference quotient for vector-valued functions, computed component-wise.
hint: Differentiate each component function (i, j, k) separately using single-variable calculus rules.

description: The derivative vector r'(t) that points in the direction of motion along a space curve.
hint: Evaluate the derivative at the specific parameter value to get the tangent vector at that point.

description: The normalized tangent vector T(t) = r'(t)/||r'(t)|| with magnitude one.
hint: Divide the tangent vector by its own magnitude to get a direction without considering speed.

description: Second and higher derivatives r''(t), r'''(t) obtained by repeatedly differentiating component-wise.
hint: Apply the derivative operator multiple times to each component function independently.

description: The antiderivative of a vector-valued function, computed component-wise with constant vectors.
hint: Integrate each component separately and add a constant vector C with arbitrary components.

description: The integral from a to b of r(t)dt, evaluated component-wise using fundamental theorem.
hint: Integrate each component from a to b separately, then combine into a single vector result.

description: Product rules for scalar-vector, dot product, and cross product derivatives of vector functions.
hint: For dot and cross products, apply product rule but preserve order for cross products (non-commutative).

description: A space curve where r'(t) is continuous and nonzero on its domain.
hint: Check that the derivative vector exists everywhere and never becomes the zero vector.

description: The distance along a smooth curve from one point to another, computed using the integral of the magnitude of the velocity vector.
hint: Start by finding the derivative r'(t), then compute its magnitude, and integrate over the interval.

description: A special parameterization s of a curve where distance along the curve equals the parameter value, making ||r'(s)|| = 1.
hint: To convert to arc length parameter, compute s(t) = ∫[a,t] ||r'(u)|| du, then solve for t(s) and substitute.

description: A measure of how sharply a curve bends at a given point, denoted κ (kappa), representing the rate of change of the unit tangent vector with respect to arc length.
hint: Curvature is zero for straight lines and larger for tighter curves; think about how the tangent vector direction changes.

description: The formula κ = ||dT/ds|| = ||T'(t)||/||r'(t)|| or equivalently κ = ||r'(t) × r''(t)||/||r'(t)||³ for computing curvature.
hint: Use the cross product formula when working with explicit parameterizations to avoid computing arc length parameter.

description: The unit vector N(t) = T'(t)/||T'(t)|| that points in the direction the curve is turning, perpendicular to the tangent.
hint: First find the unit tangent T, differentiate it, then normalize the result; N always points toward the concave side of the curve.

description: The unit vector B(t) = T(t) × N(t) that is perpendicular to both the tangent and normal vectors, completing the TNB frame.
hint: Use the cross product of T and N; the binormal is automatically a unit vector since T and N are orthononal unit vectors.

description: The plane containing the unit tangent and principal unit normal vectors at a point on a curve, representing the plane that best approximates the curve locally.
hint: The osculating plane has the binormal vector B as its normal vector; it's where the curve appears to lie instantaneously.

description: The radius ρ = 1/κ of the osculating circle that best approximates the curve at a given point.
hint: Larger radius means gentler curve; when curvature is small, the radius of curvature is large.

description: A vector-valued function r(t) that describes the location of a particle in space at time t.
hint: Write r(t) with components for x, y, and z coordinates, each as a function of time.

description: The derivative of the position vector r'(t) = v(t), representing instantaneous velocity with direction and magnitude.
hint: Differentiate each component of the position vector separately to find velocity.

description: The derivative of velocity v'(t) = a(t), or the second derivative of position r''(t), representing rate of change of velocity.
hint: Take the derivative of the velocity vector, or the second derivative of position.

description: The magnitude of the velocity vector ||v(t)||, a scalar representing how fast the particle moves.
hint: Calculate the magnitude of the velocity vector using the square root of the sum of squared components.

description: Motion under constant gravitational acceleration, described by position vector with quadratic vertical component and linear horizontal components.
hint: Use initial position and velocity vectors, then add the acceleration term -½gt²k to the vertical component.

id: Tangential and Normal Components of Acceleration
description: Decomposition of acceleration into aₜ (tangent to path, changes speed) and aₙ (normal to path, changes direction).
hint: The tangential component aₜ = v'(t) changes speed; the normal component aₙ = κv² changes direction using curvature.

description: Three laws describing orbital motion: elliptical orbits, equal area in equal time, and period-distance relationship.
hint: Apply conservation of angular momentum and inverse-square gravitational force to derive orbital properties.

description: A function that takes two or more independent variables as input and produces a single real number output, written as f(x,y) or f(x,y,z).
hint: Think of temperature at different locations: each point (x,y) on a map has one temperature value.
chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

description: The set of all ordered pairs or triples for which a multivariable function is defined and produces real output values.
hint: Check where denominators are zero or where expressions under square roots become negative, then exclude those points.
chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

description: The set of all possible output values that a multivariable function can produce from its domain.
hint: Find the minimum and maximum values the function can take by analyzing its behavior over its entire domain.
chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

description: A surface in three-dimensional space representing all points (x,y,z) where z equals f(x,y).
hint: Plot points by choosing (x,y) values in the domain and computing z = f(x,y), then connect them to visualize the surface.
chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

description: Curves in the xy-plane where a function f(x,y) equals a constant value k, obtained by setting f(x,y) = k.
hint: Like contour lines on a topographic map, connect all points at the same 'height' or function value.
chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

description: Surfaces in three-dimensional space where a function f(x,y,z) equals a constant value k, obtained by setting f(x,y,z) = k.
hint: For each constant k, solve f(x,y,z) = k to get a surface; different k values produce a family of surfaces.
chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

description: The curve obtained by intersecting the graph of f(x,y) with a vertical plane such as x = a or y = b.
hint: Hold one variable constant and graph the resulting single-variable function to see a cross-section of the surface.
chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

description: A function w = f(x,y,z) that assigns a real number to each point in three-dimensional space within its domain.
hint: Since we cannot visualize four dimensions directly, use level surfaces f(x,y,z) = k to understand the function's behavior.
chapter: Differentiation of Functions of Several Variables

description: The value L that f(x,y) approaches as (x,y) approaches (a,b) along all possible paths.
